There is no minimum amount of NIM that must be pre-staked, but 80% of the pre-staked NIM must signal readiness during an activation window. If this threshold is not met, a new activation window will start immediately after the current one ends. Each new activation window spans 1440 PoW blocks, equivalent to one day, and this process will continue until the required readiness threshold is reached. You can simply start your PoS client once the migration process ends and build your own node."],["p",{},"It''s important to note that, as a node operator, your role is primarily as an observer during the activation process. The migration will only proceed if at least 80% of the allocated stake is ready to migrate. Since you have not pre-registered as a validator, running the activation tool does not contribute to meet the threshold requirement for the transition. Nonetheless, by running the migration tool, you will replicate the state migration and genesis block creation process."],["h2",{"id":"activation-tool"},"Activation Tool"],["p",{},"The Activation Tool facilitates the transition from the PoW chain to the PoS chain. During this phase, you will create and register your validator on the PoW Mainnet, preparing it for the transition to PoS."],["p",{},"Nimiq has developed a ",["strong",{},"Validator Registration Tool"]," specifically for this process. By using this tool, validators pre-register within the PoW chain, ensuring they are ready for the PoS transition. The tool operates in two modes:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Key and Address Generation"],": Generates the validator''s keys and corresponding address"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Validator Registration"],": Registers the validator by sending the necessary transactions on the PoW chain"]],["table",{},["thead",{},["tr",{},["th",{},["strong",{},"Phase"]],["th",{},["strong",{},"Start Date"]],["th",{},["strong",{},"End Date"]]]],["tbody",{},["tr",{},["td",{},["strong",{},"Validator Registration"]],["td",{},["strong",{},"12th September"]],["td",{},["strong",{},"6th steps outlined in this guide are only applicable during the ",["strong",{},"Validator Registration Phase"],"."],["h3",{"id":"prerequisites"},"Prerequisites"],["ul",{},["li",{},"Ensure repository"]],["h3",{"id":"step-1-generate-the-validator-keys"},"Step 1: Generate the Validator Keys"],["p",{},"Execute the tool without parameters to generate a validator address, signing key, and voting key:"],["pre",{"className":"language-bash shiki shiki-themes material-theme-lighter material-theme material-theme-palenight","code":"node validator-registration.js\n","language":"bash","meta":"","style":""},["code",{"__ignoreMap":""},["span",{"class":"line","line":1},["span",{"class":"sBMFI"},"node"],["span",{"class":"sfazB"}," validator-registration.js\n"]]]],["p",{},"The tool generates fresh keys and stores them into the ",["code",{},"validator-keys.json"]," file in your current directory. The screenshot demonstrates an example output of the script:"],["img",{"className":["object-contain","max-h-[max(80vh,220px)]"],"src":"/assets/images/migration/migration.png","alt":"Validator example keys"}],["callout",{"icon":"i-tabler-bulb"},"Save the private keys securely, especially the validator private key! ",["strong",{},"There is no recovery mechanism for lost private keys"],". This will print out the usage instructions."],["p",{},["strong",{},"Using your own keys"]],["p",{},"You can of course also generate your own validator, signing, and/or voting keys using other methods. Once you have added your keys, continue with the next step."],["h3",{"id":"step-2-fund-your-validator-address"},"Step 2: Fund your Validator Address"],["p",{},"To start the validator registration process, fund the validator address you just generated to cover the nominal transaction fees of 1 Luna each associated with the validator registration process. During the ",["a",{"href":"../migration/migration-technical-details#readiness-and-activation-tool"},"Activation Phase"],", you will also need to pay the readiness transaction, so ensure your address is funded with at least ",["strong",{},"1 NIM"]," for both the registration and activation transactions. You can use any Nimiq Wallet to send NIM to this address."],["h3",{"id":"step-3-run-the-validator-registration-tool"},"Step 3: Run the Validator Registration Tool"],["p",{},"The Validator Registration Tool connects to the Nimiq PoW chain when executed. It registers your validator by importing the ",["strong",{},"generated keys"]," and then creates and sends ",["strong",{},"six transactions"],", each with a ",["strong",{},"nominal fee of 1 Luna"],". These transactions use your validator keys to sign and submit the necessary data to register your validator."],["p",{},"Run the following command from the cloned ",["code",{},"validator-registration-tool"]," repository. You must specify the arguments as follows:"],["pre",{"className":"language-bash shiki shiki-themes material-theme-lighter material-theme material-theme-palenight","code":"node validator-registration.js --validator validator-keys.json --network main\n","language":"bash","meta":"","style":""},["code",{"__ignoreMap":""},["span",{"class":"line","line":1},["span",{"class":"sBMFI"},"node"],["span",{"class":"sfazB"}," validator-registration.js"],["span",{"class":"sfazB"}," --validator"],["span",{"class":"sfazB"}," validator-keys.json"],["span",{"class":"sfazB"}," --network"],["span",{"class":"sfazB"}," main\n"]]]],["h3",{"id":"step-4-deposit-payment-and-commit"},"Step 4: Deposit Payment and Commit"],["p",{},"The final step involves committing to the registration and paying the validator''s deposit of 100 000 NIM. This transaction can be sent from any address, but ",["strong",{},"you must include your validator address"]," in the transaction’s “public message” field. This allows the deposit to be linked to your validator."],["p",{},"To send the transaction manually via ",["strong",{},"Nimiq Wallet"],", you need the following data:"],["table",{},["thead",{},["tr",{},["th",{}],["th",{}]]],["tbody",{},["tr",{},["td",{},["strong",{},"Recipient Address"]],["td",{},["code",{},"NQ07 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000"]]],["tr",{},["td",{},["strong",{},"Value"]],["td",{},["code",{},"100 000"]," NIM or more"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["strong",{},"Public Message"]],["td",{},"Your validator address in human-readable format (starting with ",["code",{},"NQ"],")"]]]],["callout",{"icon":"i-tabler-alert-triangle","color":"warning"},["p",{},["strong",{},"We recommend using the Nimiq Wallet"]," to send the validator deposit, as it allows you to include the necessary public message. If you are using an exchange or another wallet, be aware that they often don’t allow public messages on-chain for withdrawals. To ensure everything works as expected, first send a small transaction (2 Lunas) between your own addresses with a simple message (like \"hello world\") to verify that the message is included properly. You can verify the address included the message with a block explorer like ",["a",{"href":"https://nimiq.watch","rel":["nofollow"]},"nimiq.watch"],"."],["p",{},"Ensure you follow these steps carefully, as failure to do so could result in permanent loss of your funds. Any value below 100 000 NIM will also result in permanent loss. Any amount above 100 000 NIM will be assigned as stake as long as the difference is greater than the minimum stake (100 NIM); otherwise, the excess will be burned."]],["p",{},["strong",{},"Verify Registration and Deposit"]],["p",{},"After the command runs successfully and the deposit is sent, you can verify both on the network using our NIM block explorer ",["a",{"href":"https://nimiq.watch/#validator-registrations","rel":["nofollow"]},"Nimiq Watch"],"."],["h3",{"id":"whats-next"},"What’s Next?"],["p",{},"Congratulations! This could be a dApp, payment platform, or any web application."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Hub"],": The user-facing interface that displays transaction details, manages accounts, and coordinates between your app and the Keyguard. The Hub validates requests and presents them to users in a friendly UI."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Keyguard"],": The secure, isolated component that holds private keys and performs all cryptographic operations. It''s designed with maximum security in mind and never exposes private keys to the Hub or your application."]],["callout",{"icon":"i-tabler-info-circle","color":"info"},["p",{},["strong",{},"Security by Design"]],["p",{},"The Keyguard runs in a separate origin and iframe with strict security policies. Private keys are encrypted with user passwords and never leave the Keyguard environment. Flow"],["code-group",{},["pre",{"className":"language-ts shiki shiki-themes material-theme-lighter material-theme material-theme-palenight","code":"// 1. User clicks \"Sign In\"\nasync function signIn() {\n  try {\n    const challenge = generateRandomChallenge() // Server-provided nonce\n\n    const result = await hubApi.signMessage({\n      appName: ''My App'',\n      message: `Sign in to My App\\nChallenge: ${challenge}`,\n    })\n\n    // 2. Send to server for verification\n    const response = await fetch(''/api/auth/verify'', {\n      method: ''POST'',\n      headers: { ''Content-Type'': ''application/json'' },\n      body: JSON.stringify({\n        address: result.signer,\n        signature: Array.from(result.signature),\n        publicKey: Array.from(result.signerPublicKey),\n        challenge,\n      }),\n    })\n\n    const { token } = await response.json()\n\n    // 3. Store auth token\n    localStorage.setItem(''authToken'', token)\n    navigateTo(''/dashboard'')\n  }\n  catch (error) {\n    showError(''Sign in failed'')\n  }\n}\n\n// Server-side verification (Node.js)\napp.post(''/api/auth/verify'', (req, res) => {\n  const { address, signature, publicKey, challenge } = req.body\n\n  const message = `Sign in to My App\\nChallenge: ${challenge}`\n\n  if (verifySignedMessage(message, signature, publicKey)) {\n    const token = generateJWT({ address })\n    res.json({ token })\n  }\n  else {\n    res.status(401).json({ error: ''Invalid signature'' })\n  }\n})\n","filename":"sign-in-authentication.ts","language":"ts","meta":"","style":""},["code",{"__ignoreMap":""},["span",{"class":"line","line":1},["span",{"class":"sHwdD"},"// 1. INSERT INTO _content_docs VALUES ('docs/migration/migration-integrators.md', 'Guide for Integrators and Exchanges', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["h3",{"id":"general-considerations-for-different-node-options"},"General Considerations for Different Node Options"],["p",{},"We have the following node types:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"History nodes"]," (recommend): Stores all transactions since genesis but ",["strong",{},"does not permanently retain all block data"],". However, transactions are never pruned and remain retrievable by their hash and block number."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Full nodes"],": Holds all the blocks and transactions of only roughly the last 24 hours. The configuration of this duration is explained later in this document via the ",["code",{},"client.toml"],"."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Light nodes"],": This node type is ",["strong",{},"not"]," suitable for integrators, as it lacks capabilities like a mempool, transaction inclusion and block bodies. It is meant to run on low-end devices like mobile phones and browsers."]],["h3",{"id":"hardware-requirements-and-recommendations-per-node-type"},"Hardware Requirements and Recommendations per Node Type"],["table",{},["thead",{},["tr",{},["th",{},"PoS Node Type"],["th",{},"Memory"],["th",{},"CPU"],["th",{},"Storage"],["th",{},"Network"],["th",{},"Syncing Time"]]],["tbody",{},["tr",{},["td",{},"History (recommend)"],["td",{},"Minimum 16GB RAM (higher recommended)"],["td",{},"Minimum 4 vCPUs, 8 recommended"],["td",{},"Starts from a few gigabytes, grows linearly with blockchain size"],["td",{},"High-speed, reliable internet connection; Good I/O performance (SSDs required)"],["td",{},"Sync time increases over the life of the blockchain"]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Full"],["td",{},"Minimum 8GB RAM"],["td",{},"Minimum 4 vCPUs, 8 recommended"],["td",{},"Minimum 80GB of storage, 160GB recommended"],["td",{},"High-speed, reliable internet connection; Good I/O performance (SSDs recommended)"],["td",{},"Sync time grows linearly but slowly"]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Light"],["td",{},"1GB RAM recommended"],["td",{},"64-bit recommended"],["td",{},"Works with minimal storage"],["td",{},"Moderate-speed internet connection (e.g., 1 Mbps or higher)"],["td",{},"Syncs in a few seconds"]]]],["h3",{"id":"json-rpc-interface"},"JSON-RPC Interface"],["p",{},"The JSON-RPC interface provides methods for generating addresses, creating/sending transactions, retrieving balances and much more. The full specification is available here: ",["a",{"href":"/rpc/methods/"},"PoS JSON-RPC Specification"],"."],["h3",{"id":"key-json-rpc-methods"},"Key JSON-RPC Methods"],["p",{},"The following methods are particularly useful when interacting with the network:"],["ol",{},["li",{},["a",{"href":"/rpc/methods/get-transaction-by-hash"},["code",{},"getTransactionByHash"]],": Retrieve transaction details using a transaction hash."],["li",{},["a",{"href":"/rpc/methods/get-transaction-hashes-by-address"},["code",{},"getTransactionHashesByAddress"]],": Fetch transaction hashes associated with a given address."],["li",{},["a",{"href":"/rpc/methods/get-block-number"},["code",{},"getBlockNumber"]],": Returns the current head block of the node."],["li",{},["a",{"href":"/rpc/methods/get-block-by-number"},["code",{},"getBlockByNumber"]],": Returns the block at the specified height"],["li",{},["a",{"href":"/rpc/methods/get-raw-transaction-info"},["code",{},"getRawTransactionInfo"]],": Get raw transaction details by decoding a serialized transaction."],["li",{},["a",{"href":"/rpc/methods/is-consensus-established"},["code",{},"isConsensusEstablished"]],": Returns a boolean specifying if the node has established consensus with the network."],["li",{},["a",{"href":"/rpc/methods/create-basic-transaction"},["code",{},"createBasicTransaction"]],": Create a signed transaction and returns a hex-encoded representation without broadcasting it. All ",["code",{},"create*Transaction"]," RPC methods also have an equivalent ",["code",{},"send*Transaction"]," version in order to create and broadcast the transaction one-go."],["li",{},["a",{"href":"/rpc/methods/send-raw-transaction"},["code",{},"sendRawTransaction"]],": Sends the given serialized and signed transaction to the network."]],["h3",{"id":"pow-account-and-transaction-history"},"PoW Account and Transaction history"],["p",{},"Nimiq has transitioned from a PoW system to a PoS system. For those needing access to historical transaction data before the PoS genesis block, a read-only database of the PoW chain is available and can be queried using the ",["a",{"href":"#json-rpc-interface"},"JSON-RPC interface"],"."],["p",{},"Pre-genesis transactions use a different format compared to PoS transactions. Instructions on how to handle and query these pre-genesis transactions will be provided in a future update."],["h2",{"id":"getting-started-with-the-pos-node"},"Getting Started with the PoS Node"],["p",{},"This section focuses on how to set up and configure your PoS node. The most important configuration settings are outlined below, but it is strongly recommended to review all the sections and apply them based on your requirements. Currently, two methods are supported for running a node: via Docker or by compiling from source code."],["h3",{"id":"the-nimiq-client"},"The ",["code",{},"nimiq-client"]],["p",{},"The ",["code",{},"nimiq-client"]," is the central software for operating a PoS node and connecting to the blockchain. Written in Rust, it is completely ",["a",{"href":"https://github.com/nimiq/core-rs-albatross","rel":["nofollow"]},"open source"],". While we make no guarantees about the minimum supported Rust version, we currently test two versions older than the current Rust stable version. Releases follow ",["a",{"href":"https://semver.org/","rel":["nofollow"]},"Semantic Versioning"]," rules, meaning patch releases are non-breaking. If you are compiling from source we therefore ",["strong",{},"recommend"]," to use the ",["a",{"href":"https://github.com/nimiq/core-rs-albatross/releases","rel":["nofollow"]},"Github releases"]," rather than the main branch, as the latter may include breaking changes. A new Docker image is automatically uploaded to the ",["a",{"href":"https://github.com/nimiq/core-rs-albatross/pkgs/container/core-rs-albatross","rel":["nofollow"]},"Github Container registry"]," with every new release."],["h3",{"id":"the-clienttoml"},"The ",["code",{},"client.toml"]],["p",{},"The ",["code",{},"client.toml"]," is the configuration file for the ",["code",{},"nimiq-client"],". It is divided into multiple sections, and most of the properties have default values. Upon running the ",["code",{},"nimiq-client"]," for the first time, an example file is generated by the client and stored at ",["code",{},"~/.nimiq/client.example.toml"],". Rename this file to ",["code",{},"client.toml"],",  as ",["code",{},"~/.nimiq/client.toml"]," is the default location where the ",["code",{},"nimiq-client"]," will look for configurations. The example file of the ",["code",{},"client.toml"]," is available here: ",["a",{"href":"https://github.com/nimiq/core-rs-albatross/blob/albatross/lib/src/config/config_file/client.example.toml","rel":["nofollow"]},"GitHub"],"."],["h3",{"id":"important-configuration-file-settings"},"Important Configuration File Settings"],["p",{},"When setting up a node, ensure the following settings are properly configured in your ",["code",{},"client.toml"],". Every property has either a default value or accepted options. The following list highlights the most important ones to get your node up and running:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"network.seed_nodes:"]," Depending on whether you are connecting to the PoS Testnet or Mainnet, the relevant seed nodes need to be provided. A list per network is provided ",["a",{"href":"#mainnet-and-testnet-seed-nodes"},"later"]," in this document."],["li",{},["strong",{},"network.tls:"]," TLS is ",["strong",{},"optional"],"; the client uses the ",["a",{"href":"https://libp2p.io","rel":["nofollow"]},"libp2p"]," network stack under the hood which enforces encrypted connections between peers out-of-the-box."],["li",{},["strong",{},"consensus.network"],": Set to ",["code",{},"main-albatross"]," in order to connect to the PoS Mainnet (default is Testnet)."],["li",{},["strong",{},"consensus.sync_mode"],": ",["strong",{},"Must"]," be ",["code",{},"history"]," or ",["code",{},"full"]," as outlined in ",["a",{"href":"#general-considerations-for-different-node-options"},"General Considerations"],"."],["li",{},["strong",{},"consensus.max_epochs_stored:"]," Only applies if ",["code",{},"consensus.sync_mode"]," is set to ",["code",{},"full"],"; configures how long blocks and transactions are kept around before they get pruned (1 epoch translates roughly to 12 hours)."],["li",{},["strong",{},"rpc-server"],": Uncomment the JSON-RPC server section (which it is by default) and configure the bind address and port for remote communication if desired. However, it is not recommended to allow communication outside of localhost unless TLS is used."]],["h3",{"id":"mainnet-and-testnet-seed-nodes"},"Mainnet and Testnet Seed Nodes"],["p",{},"Both the PoS Mainnet and Testnet have their own seed nodes for initial connection and network discovery within the respective blockchain network. Window"],["p",{},"The blockchain has a  ",["strong",{},"~2 hours"]," validity window, meaning that a transaction is valid to be included in a block within that timeframe after the transaction''s validity start height. If a transaction is not included within this window, it becomes invalid and will be rejected by the network."],["h3",{"id":"transaction-failure"},["strong",{},"Transaction Failure"]],["p",{},"Transactions can fail for multiple reasons, such as missing information or invalid parameters. Before a transaction is added to the blockchain, it must meet all the conditions. If any of the following errors occur, the transaction will be rejected by the network and not be included in a block:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"NoSender"],": The sender address is missing from the transaction."],["li",{},["strong",{},"NoRecipient"],": The recipient address is missing. A valid recipient must be provided."],["li",{},["strong",{},"NoValue"],": The transaction value (amount to be transferred) is missing."],["li",{},["strong",{},"NoValidityStartHeight"],": The transaction’s validity start height, which defines from when the transaction becomes valid, is not set."],["li",{},["strong",{},"NoNetworkId"],": The transaction’s network ID is missing. The ID identifies which network the transaction is intended, whether Testnet or Mainnet."]],["h3",{"id":"transaction-finality"},"Transaction Finality"],["p",{},"Once a valid transaction is included in a ",["strong",{},"micro block"]," (blocks containing user-generated transactions), finality is achieved through ",["strong",{},"macro blocks"],". Macro blocks are produced after 59 micro blocks (~1 minute) using the Tendermint consensus algorithm. Once a macro block is added to the chain, all the transactions from the previous 59 micro blocks are finalized and have become irreversible."],["h2",{"id":"reference"},"Reference"],["ul",{},["li",{},"Albatross White Paper: General"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},["code",{},"params"]," property in requests is now required"],["br",{}],"\nWhile in PoW the ",["code",{},"params"]," property in requests could be left out for requests that didn''t take any parameters (or only optional ones), this is no longer the case in PoS. At least an empty array (",["code",{},"[]"],") must now be passed."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Most params are now required"],["br",{}],"\nIn PoW many parameters could be left out, because they had a default in the server. In PoS most parameters are now required. For non-required parameters, ",["code",{},"null"]," must still be passed in their place to use the server''s default."],["li",{},["strong",{},"The result of a JSON-RPC response is in ",["code",{},"result.data"]],["br",{}],"\nIn PoS, every RPC response contains ",["code",{},"result: { data, metadata }"],". INSERT INTO _content_docs VALUES ('docs/migration/migration-technical-details.md', 'Migration Technical Details', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["p",{},"Each phase operates within a specified block window, enabling transactions to be sent to the network at specific points within the PoW chain."],["p",{},"Our approach consists of targeting a specific block height within the PoW chain designated as the ",["em",{},"transition block"],". At this block height, the blockchain state will be captured and used to generate the \"genesis block\" for the PoS chain. Instead of starting from block height 0, the PoS chain will start from the same block number as the transition block, if the conditions covered in this document are met."],["callout",{"color":"info","icon":"i-tabler-info-circle"},"Quotes are used around \"genesis block\" because it refers to the first block of the PoS chain, though not block 0, as is typical for a genesis block. It is also called the transition block since it marks the shift from PoW to PoS."],["h2",{"id":"validator-registration-phase"},"Validator Registration Phase"],["p",{},"The transition begins by establishing the first validator list for the PoS blockchain within the PoW blockchain. This phase is open to all users, and being a miner is not a prerequisite for registering as a validator. Users must complete a series of transactions to indicate their willingness to become validators."],["p",{},"To become a validator, users need a validator account, a signing key, and a voting key. Additionally, users must pay a minimum deposit of 100 000 NIM. Any amount exceeding the deposit contributes to the validator''s stake as long as it exceeds the minimum deposit for stakers of 100 NIM."],["p",{},"This phase involves a series of six transactions to register the validator keys and an additional one to confirm by paying the validator deposit. Users must complete the entire sequence of transactions to have their validator included in the PoS \"genesis\" block. NIM spent on incomplete registrations will be burned."],["p",{},"Nimiq provides a two-mode tool to generate validator keys and send formatted registration transactions to the blockchain. Please refer to ",["a",{"href":"../archive/validator-registration"},"our guide"]," to learn how to use the tool and pre-register."],["h3",{"id":"registration-transactions"},"Registration Transactions"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Validator Registration:"]," The first 6 transactions contain the encoded validator keys. Each transaction must be sent from the account of the registering validator, and have a value of 1 Luna each."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Deposit Payment and Commitment:"]," The final step of this phase is to pay the validator deposit of 100 000 NIM and commit to the registration. Unlike the other six transactions, this one can be sent from any account, but the data field must include the registered validator''s address to identify the validator."]],["p",{},"The validator generation transactions include the following data:"],["table",{},["thead",{},["tr",{},["th",{},"Type"],["th",{},"Data"]]],["tbody",{},["tr",{},["td",{},"Sender"],["td",{},"Validator address"]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Recipient"],["td",{},"Burn address"]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Value"],["td",{},"0.00001 NIM"]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Data"],["td",{},"Validator keys encoded in 64 bytes, see below"]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Validity"],["td",{},"Registration block window"]]]],["p",{},"Due to the 64-byte data limit of a regular Nimiq transaction, validator keys are split across six transactions. The initial transaction transmits both the signing key and the validator''s address. Subsequent transactions are used to progressively send the remainder of the data, particularly the voting key. In addition, each transaction is numbered (1-6). This process ensures the secure transmission and reconstruction of the complete validator keys. Mind that only the public keys are included in these transactions. The private key components are meant to be stored safely."],["p",{},"The registration window takes place within a specific range of blocks. The count of validators and their deposits is made at the last block of this phase."],["p",{},["img",{"alt":"Structure of validator registration transaction data","src":"/assets/images/protocol/migration-txs.png"}]],["h2",{"id":"pre-staking-phase"},"Pre-staking Phase"],["p",{},"The Pre-Staking phase occurs within a specific range of blocks and is exclusive for staking. During this phase, validator registration transactions are no longer accepted."],["p",{},"Users who wish to stake their NIM for the PoS chain launch can send a pre-stake transaction of a minimum of 100 NIM, specifying which pre-registered validator they want to stake with. Any attempt to stake with non-registered validators or incorrect transaction details will result in the loss of the sent NIM. It is critical to follow the instructions exactly to avoid losing funds. Please note that once NIMs are pre-staked for a pre-registered validator, unstaking is not possible in the PoW chain. The stake can only be accessed once the PoS chain begins."],["p",{},"Pre-staking can be done through the ",["a",{"href":"https://wallet.nimiq.com/","rel":["nofollow"]},"Nimiq Wallet"],", which features an interface specifically designed to facilitate this. The Nimiq Wallet will provide users with a list of pre-registered validators and pools, allow them to select one, and send their pre-staking transaction. Every Address can only pre-stake for one pre-registered validator."],["p",{},"After the pre-staking phase, a clear overview of the registered validators and the total allocated stake will be provided."],["h2",{"id":"activation-phase"},"Activation Phase"],["p",{},"Only pre-registered validators can participate in this phase. The activation phase serves the purpose of executing the transition, planned to start approximately on ",["strong",{},"November 19th, 2024"],". At the first candidate block''s height (block 3''456''000), if at least 80% of the total stake signals readiness for the transition, the candidate block becomes the transition block. A special activation tool captures and migrates the state of the PoW chain at the transition block, including the transaction history, to generate the \"genesis block\" for the new PoS chain."],["p",{},"The activation phase follows a specific sequence of events, outlined in the following list. The overall process will be explained in more detail afterward."],["ol",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"3 days"]," before November 19th, validators running the activation tool will begin sending automatic ",["strong",{},"online transactions"]," every hour before the first candidate block is mined to signal their online status."],["li",{},"The candidate block is mined at the block height 3''456''000, officially opening the first activation window."],["li",{},"The network waits for 10 confirmations after the candidate block to prevent block reversion."],["li",{},"After the 10 confirmations, pre-registered validators capture the state at block 3,456,000 and begin generating the PoS \"genesis\" block. Validators generate the \"genesis\" block deterministically. The block hash generated should be the same across all validators."],["li",{},"After generating the \"genesis\" block, validators send their readiness transactions, which include the block hash in the data field."],["li",{},"The activation tool monitors readiness transactions within a 24-hour window."],["li",{},"If 80% of the total stake (represented by registered validators) sends readiness transactions within 24 hours, the PoS chain starts at block height 3,456,000, disregarding any blocks mined after this point."],["li",{},"If the 80% readiness threshold is not met within the first 24 hours, a new activation window begins, starting with a new candidate block 1,440 PoW blocks (~24 hours) after the first."],["li",{},"Validators who do not send readiness transactions by the ",["strong",{},"5th activation window"]," will no longer be considered part of the readiness voting process and will be deactivated from the first epoch of the PoS chain. This process ensures that inactive validators are removed from consideration to the required threshold."],["li",{},"The activation windows will continue every 24 hours until 80% readiness is achieved. Validators who fail to signal readiness in subsequent windows will be progressively removed until the required threshold is reached, ensuring that only active validators participate in the PoS chain when it starts."],["li",{},"Once 80% readiness is reached, the PoS client starts, and the once-candidate block becomes the transition block, marking the start of the Nimiq PoS blockchain."]],["h3",{"id":"online-transactions-and-monitoring"},"Online Transactions and Monitoring"],["p",{},"To ensure a smooth activation, pre-registered validators are encouraged to run the activation tool at least 3 days before November 19th. Once the tool is launched, it will send automatic online transactions every hour (costing 1 Luna each) starting 3 days before the first candidate block is mined to signal that the validator is online. These transactions continue until the validator sends the readiness transaction at block height 3''456''000. Validators who run the tool earlier will only start sending these automatic online transactions 3 days before activation."],["p",{},"The purpose of these online transactions is to allow Team Nimiq to monitor which validators are online and ready for the transition. Validators will receive 100 Luna from Team Nimiq to cover the cost of these online and readiness transactions. These transactions help ensure all validators are ready and provide the team with a way to contact those who haven’t shown they are prepared."],["h3",{"id":"readiness-transactions"},"Readiness Transactions"],["p",{},"Once the first activation window opens, validators send a transaction signaling their readiness. Pre-registered validators are encouraged to run the activation tool before the phase begins, as migrating the entire transaction history is time-consuming. An activation tool has been developed to facilitate this process. The tool migrates the history and scans the PoW chain for readiness transactions, selecting the valid ones that come from pre-registered validators who have completed the registration process. The tool also verifies the ",["code",{},"data"]," field to ensure the genesis hash that validators signaled readiness for matches the candidate transition block generated."],["p",{},"The readiness transaction is as follows:"],["table",{},["thead",{},["tr",{},["th",{},"Type"],["th",{},"Data"]]],["tbody",{},["tr",{},["td",{},"Sender"],["td",{},"Validator address"]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Recipient"],["td",{},"Burn address"]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Value"],["td",{},"0.00001 NIM"]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Data"],["td",{},"Hash of the generated ",["code",{},"GenesisConfig"]]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Validity"],["td",{},"Activation block window"]]]],["h3",{"id":"transition-block"},"Transition Block"],["p",{},"The ",["code",{},"GenesisConfig"]," is generated after the candidate block is mined. If the pre-registered validators run the tool before the activation window starts, the tool will have already migrated most of the history by the time the candidate block is mined. After the candidate block is mined and the network reaches 80% readiness, the tool migrates the final part of the history and generates the \"genesis\" block, which becomes the candidate transition block for the PoS chain."],["p",{},"The activation process activates at the candidate block. At this point, the tool scans and analyzes transactions sent to the PoW chain. The transition starts if at least 80% of the total stake is ready for the specified candidate transition block."],["h3",{"id":"ensuring-migration-completion"},"Ensuring Migration Completion"],["p",{},"If the transition does not succeed in the initial activation window, a new window opens immediately, starting from the block after 1,440 PoW blocks (~24 hours). This process repeats every 24 hours until the 80% readiness threshold is reached, or until the 5th window, at which point unready validators will be marked as inactive and will not count towards the 80% readiness threshold."],["h4",{"id":"process-breakdown"},"Process Breakdown:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"First Activation Window"],": The process begins on November 19th with the first activation window. This window lasts 24 hours, during which validators must send readiness transactions to reach the 80% readiness threshold."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Subsequent Windows (2–5)"],": If the 80% threshold is not met during the first window, new activation windows will open every 24 hours, starting with a new candidate block. This process continues for up to 5 windows. Readiness can be achieved in any of these windows, in which case the PoS chain will start immediately."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Evaluation at the End of Window 5"],": If readiness is still not achieved by the end of the 5th window, validators who have not sent their readiness transactions will be marked as inactive and won''t be considered for the 80% readiness threshold. This ensures that only active validators are considered for the migration."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Window 6 and Onward"],": Starting from window 6, we expect to reach 80% readiness more quickly, as unready validators will have been marked as inactive. This allows the migration process to progress faster, ensuring the PoS chain can launch as soon as possible."]],["p",{},"This process guarantees the migration will succeed by excluding inactive validators, ensuring that only active participants contribute to the readiness threshold. By marking unready validators as inactive, the system ensures that the PoS chain can start efficiently. This mechanism is designed to secure completion of the migration, even if initial readiness is not achieved."],["h2",{"id":"nimiq-pos"},"Nimiq PoS"],["p",{},"Once 80% readiness is achieved, the PoS chain begins. Any subsequent transaction included in the PoW chain after the candidate transition block is not considered part of the PoS chain. From this point forward, the network operates entirely under the PoS consensus mechanism."]],"toc":{"title":"","searchDepth":2,"depth":2,"links":[{"id":"validator-registration-phase","depth":2,"text":"Validator Registration Phase","children":[{"id":"registration-transactions","depth":3,"text":"Registration Transactions"}]},{"id":"pre-staking-phase","depth":2,"text":"Pre-staking Phase"},{"id":"activation-phase","depth":2,"text":"Activation Phase","children":[{"id":"online-transactions-and-monitoring","depth":3,"text":"Online Transactions and Monitoring"},{"id":"readiness-transactions","depth":3,"text":"Readiness Transactions"},{"id":"transition-block","depth":3,"text":"Transition Block"},{"id":"ensuring-migration-completion","depth":3,"text":"Ensuring Migration Completion"}]},{"id":"nimiq-pos","depth":2,"text":"Nimiq PoS"}]}}', 'Nimiq is transitioning from a Proof-of-Work (PoW) to a Proof-of-Stake (PoS) blockchain. Add the dual-chain mini app"],["p",{},"In ",["code",{},"src/App.vue"],", use separate script, template, and style blocks."],["h3",{"id":"_51-add-the-script-block"},"5.1 Add the script block"],["pre",{"className":"language-vue shiki shiki-themes material-theme-lighter material-theme material-theme-palenight","code":"<script setup lang=\"ts\">\nimport { ref } from ''vue''\n\ninterface NimiqProvider {\n  listAccounts: () => Promise<string[]>\n  sign: (message: string | { message: string, isHex: boolean }) => Promise<unknown>\n}\n\ninterface EthereumProvider {\n  request: (args: { method: string, params?: unknown[] | Record<string, unknown> }) => Promise<any>\n}\n\ndeclare global {\n  interface Window {\n    nimiq?: NimiqProvider\n    ethereum?: EthereumProvider\n  }\n}\n\nconst loading = ref(false)\nconst status = ref<string | null>(null)\nconst errorMessage = ref<string | null>(null)\n\nconst nimiqAccounts = ref<string[] | null>(null)\nconst nimiqSignature = ref<string | null>(null)\nconst ethAccounts = ref<string[] | null>(null)\nconst ethSignature = ref<string | null>(null)\n\n// Convert a UTF-8 string to hex for personal_sign.\nfunction toHexUtf8(input: string) {\n  return `0x${Array.from(new TextEncoder().encode(input))\n    .map(byte => byte.toString(16).padStart(2, ''0''))\n    .join('''')}`\n}\n\nfunction resetMessages() {\n  errorMessage.value = null\n  status.value = null\n}\n\nasync function runNimiqFlow() {\n  if (!window.nimiq) {\n    errorMessage.value = ''Nimiq provider not found. Open this app inside Nimiq Pay.''\n    return\n  }\n\n  resetMessages()\n  loading.value = true\n\n  try {\n    // Prompt 1: account sharing confirmation.\n    status.value = ''Requesting Nimiq accounts...''\n    const accounts = await window.nimiq.listAccounts()\n    nimiqAccounts.value = accounts\n    if (!accounts.length)\n      throw new Error(''No Nimiq accounts returned.'')\n\n    // Prompt 2: signing confirmation.\n    status.value = ''Requesting Nimiq signing confirmation...''\n    const signatureResult = await window.nimiq.sign(''Nimiq Pay dual-chain tutorial'')\n    nimiqSignature.value = JSON.stringify(signatureResult, null, 2)\n    status.value = ''Nimiq flow completed.''\n  }\n  catch (error) {\n    errorMessage.value = error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error)\n    status.value = ''Nimiq flow failed.''\n  }\n  finally {\n    loading.value = false\n  }\n}\n\nasync function runEthereumFlow() {\n  if (!window.ethereum) {\n    errorMessage.value = ''Ethereum provider not found. Open this app inside Nimiq Pay.''\n    return\n  }\n\n  resetMessages()\n  loading.value = true\n\n  try {\n    // Prompt 1: wallet connection confirmation.\n    status.value = ''Requesting Ethereum accounts...''\n    const accounts = await window.ethereum.request({\n      method: ''eth_requestAccounts'',\n    }) as string[]\n    ethAccounts.value = accounts\n    if (!accounts.length)\n      throw new Error(''No Ethereum accounts returned.'')\n\n    // Prompt 2: signing confirmation.\n    const message = toHexUtf8(''Nimiq Pay dual-chain tutorial'')\n    status.value = ''Requesting Ethereum signing confirmation...''\n    const signature = await window.ethereum.request({\n      method: ''personal_sign'',\n      params: [message, accounts[0]],\n    }) as string\n    ethSignature.value = signature\n    status.value = ''Ethereum flow completed.''\n  }\n  catch (error) {\n    errorMessage.value = error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error)\n    status.value = ''Ethereum flow failed.''\n  }\n  finally {\n    loading.value = false\n  }\n}\n</script>\n","language":"vue","meta":"","style":""},["code",{"__ignoreMap":""},["span",{"class":"line","line":1},["span",{"class":"sMK4o"},"<"],["span",{"class":"swJcz"},"script"],["span",{"class":"spNyl"}," Your mini app loads in the Nimiq Pay app and, when needed, can request wallet operations like listing accounts, signing messages, or sending payments, all while the user stays within the Nimiq Pay This ensures that the node has access to the blockchain data required for prover functionality. In the ",["strong",{},"consensus"]," section of the configuration file, set ",["code",{},"sync_mode"]," to ",["code",{},"full"]," as shown in the example below:"],["pre",{"className":"language-toml shiki shiki-themes material-theme-lighter material-theme material-theme-palenight","code":"[consensus]\nsync_mode = \"full\"\n","language":"toml","meta":"","style":""},["code",{"__ignoreMap":""},["span",{"class":"line","line":1},["span",{},"[consensus]\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":2},["span",{},"sync_mode = \"full\"\n"]]]],["callout",{"color":"info","icon":"i-tabler-info-circle"},"Only nodes with ",["code",{},"sync_mode"]," set to either ",["code",{},"full"]," or ",["code",{},"history"]," can become prover nodes. We recommend using ",["strong",{},"full"]," mode to ensure full prover functionality. History nodes require downloading the entire blockchain history, which is more resource-intensive."],["h2",{"id":"download-the-proving-keys"},"Download the Proving Keys"],["p",{},"Before enabling the ZK Prover in the configuration file, you need to download and place the proving keys. The validator then uses these tokens to validate transactions and produce blocks on your behalf."],["h3",{"id":"what-is-a-validator"},"What is a validator?"],["p",{},"A validator is the block producer of Proof-of-Stake blockchains, like a miner in Proof-of-Work blockchains. Validators produce blocks according to a consensus algorithm."],["h3",{"id":"what-is-a-staking-pool"},"What is a staking pool?"],["p",{},"While a validator is an individual entity, a staking pool combines the tokens of multiple participants into a single large entity, increasing their collective stake and chances of winning rewards."],["h3",{"id":"how-does-staking-help-the-nimiq-network"},"How does staking help the Nimiq network?"],["p",{},"Staking enhances the security and decentralization of the Nimiq network. Validators, backed by staked NIM, verify transactions and create new blocks. The network relies on this process to remain operational. Distributing stakes evenly across many validators reduces the risk of centralization, ensuring no single entity can control the network. This balance supports the network''s reliability, resilience, and long-term sustainability."],["h3",{"id":"what-happens-if-i-dont-stake"},"What happens if I don’t stake?"],["p",{},"You can choose not to stake and not earn any rewards. By not staking, you miss the opportunity to help maintain the network secure and decentralized."],["h3",{"id":"how-long-does-it-take-to-have-my-funds-available-after-i-un-stake"},"How long does it take to have my funds available after I un-stake?"],["p",{},"Once you un-stake your funds, it usually takes between 12 hours to 4 days for them to become available. The exact time frame depends on your validator''s status. In rare cases, validators can be \"jailed\" for up to 4 days, which may delay access to your funds."],["h2",{"id":"rewards"},"Rewards"],["h3",{"id":"how-much-can-i-earn-by-staking"},"How much can I earn by staking?"],["p",{},"The amount of NIM you will receive depends on multiple factors, such as the amount of NIM you have delegated to your validator or staking pool and the time you have been staking. In return, it earns rewards, which are shared with its stakers. You don’t need any technical knowledge to start staking."],["h3",{"id":"how-long-does-it-take-to-start-earning-rewards-after-staking"},"How long does it take to start earning rewards after staking?"],["p",{},"The periodicity of reward distribution depends on your validator policies. These rewards are paid off-chain. Check your validator to understand the specific distribution schedules and earnings."],["h3",{"id":"can-i-re-stake-my-rewards"},"Can I re-stake my rewards?"],["p",{},"Yes. If you are staking to a validator, you can manually re-stake your rewards. If you are staking with a staking pool, you can automatically set to re-stake once you earn rewards."],["h3",{"id":"i-havent-received-my-rewards-in-a-while-what-can-i-do"},"I haven’t received my rewards in a while; what can I do?"],["p",{},"If you haven’t received your rewards when you were supposed to, you can contact your validator or staking pool. According to the Nimiq PoS protocol, validators who get jailed get their funds locked for a 4 day period, which may coincide with the day of the reward distribution."],["h2",{"id":"staking-pool-selection"},"Staking Pool Selection"],["h3",{"id":"how-do-i-pick-a-staking-pool"},"How do I pick a Staking Pool?"],["p",{},"When selecting a Staking Pool, consider the following factors to make an informed decision:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Pool Fee"],": Pools charge a fee for maintaining and operating the validator infrastructure. Compare the fees of different pools to find one that aligns with your preferences."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Decentralization"],": A well-distributed stake ensures a healthy and secure Proof of Stake network. To do this, you''ll need to unstake your NIM from the current validator and then stake it with a new one. Please note that there is an unstaking period before your NIM becomes available again for staking with a different validator. This process involves two transactions: one to signal that you no longer want to stake and a second one to retrieve your funds once they are unstaked, which happens after the next macro block, a maximum of twelve hours later."],["h3",{"id":"can-i-stake-to-multiple-validators"},"Can I stake to multiple validators?"],["p",{},"You can only stake with one validator per wallet address. To stake with multiple validators, you’ll need to create additional wallet addresses. As a staker, this means you won''t earn rewards from that validator while it''s offline. However, you retain full control over your NIM and can choose to unstake and delegate to a different validator at any time. Keep in mind that unstaking involves a lock-up period before your funds become available for redelegation."],["h2",{"id":"staking-requirements"},"Staking Requirements"],["h3",{"id":"what-is-the-minimum-amount-required-to-stake"},"What is the minimum amount required to stake?"],["p",{},"To participate in staking Nimiq (NIM), a minimum of 100 NIM is required."],["h3",{"id":"i-want-to-increase-the-amount-im-staking-can-i-do-that"},"I want to increase the amount I’m staking. Validators are rewarded for validating transactions and producing blocks according to the consensus protocol, incentivizing good behavior. If a validator fails to follow the rules, they may face a punishment such as losing rewards or being temporarily “jailed” for up to 4 days, during which they cannot participate or earn rewards. This combination of rewards and punishments motivates validators to follow the rules. While validators can operate independently, staking pools allow multiple users to stake their funds with a single validator."],["p",{},"This page is intended for those operating a staking pool who wish to be listed on the ",["a",{"href":"https://wallet.nimiq.com/","rel":["nofollow"]},"Nimiq Wallet"],". By following these guidelines and submitting a complete JSON file via PR (pull request), pool operators can ensure their pool is displayed correctly in the wallet."],["h2",{"id":"general-rules"},"General Rules"],["p",{},"To maintain trust and integrity for the stakers, pool operators must follow some basic rules."],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Transparency"],": Communicate pool terms, including fees, reward timelines, and operational details. Pool operators are advised to maintain a website or any other accessible point of contact where their rules and terms are clearly outlined."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Honesty"],": Do not make misleading claims, such as advertising 0% fees while charging hidden fees."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Compliance"],": Submit a PR with your pool’s data in a JSON file following the guidelines outlined in the ",["a",{"href":"https://github.com/nimiq/validators-api?tab=readme-ov-file#README","rel":["nofollow"]},"README"],"."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Payout Commitments"],": Ensure timely and accurate payouts to stakers. Clearly define and communicate any fees to stakers. Avoid ambiguous language in your terms."]],["h2",{"id":"code-of-conduct"},"Code of Conduct"],["p",{},"All pool operators must comply with the following ethical and behavioral standards:"],["ul",{},["li",{},"Do not engage in discriminatory behavior or harassment based on race, gender, sexual orientation, religion, or other personal characteristics. No hate speech, political messaging, pornography, child abuse, etc."],["li",{},"Avoid sharing or promoting illegal content."],["li",{},"Ensure all claims about the pool, such as fees and performance metrics, are accurate and verifiable."]],["p",{},"Failure to comply with this will result in immediate actions, including potential suspension or removal from the wallet."],["h2",{"id":"submitting-your-pool-via-pr"},["strong",{},"Submitting your Pool via PR"]],["p",{},"Pool operators are required to submit a JSON file containing key details about their pool. Ensure the JSON file is complete and adheres to the guide to avoid delays in the review process. The JSON file includes fields such as:"],["ul",{},["li",{},"Pool name and description"],["li",{},"Address"],["li",{},"Fee structure (fixed or dynamic)"],["li",{},"Payout type and schedule"],["li",{},"Contact information"]],["p",{},"The review process consists of two steps:"],["ol",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"PR Review"],": When a pool submits a PR with a JSON file describing its information, someone from the Nimiq team will review it within 3 business days. This step ensures the JSON file is complete, adheres to the guidelines, and accurately describes the pool''s setup. If any required information is missing, the pool operator will be contacted to provide the details. The PR will be rejected if there is any misconduct or lack of transparency. Monitoring is currently manual, with plans to automate in the future."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Fee Verification"],": After the initial review and approval, the pool will undergo continuous monitoring to verify its fee structure and payout distributions. This is an ongoing process to ensure transparency and compliance with the stated terms."]],["h3",{"id":"links-and-references"},["strong",{},"Links and References"]],["ul",{},["li",{},["a",{"href":"https://github.com/nimiq/validators-api#add-your-validator-information","rel":["nofollow"]},"Validator Staking Pools README"]],["li",{},["a",{"href":"./becoming-a-validator"},"Become a Validator Guide"]]]],"toc":{"title":"","searchDepth":2,"depth":2,"links":[{"id":"general-rules","depth":2,"text":"General Rules"},{"id":"code-of-conduct","depth":2,"text":"Code of Conduct"},{"id":"submitting-your-pool-via-pr","depth":2,"text":"Submitting your Pool via PR","children":[{"id":"links-and-references","depth":3,"text":"Links and References"}]}]}}', 'This handbook provides guidelines for the staking pools on the Nimiq network. The value of 0.9 could represent 10% downtime, but also 20% or 5%, depending on when the downtime occurred. We use 10% to provide a relatable scale for the reader."],["h3",{"id":"availability"},"Availability"],["p",{},"The availability factor measures how often a validator is online and selected to produce blocks. Validators that are consistently selected and actively producing blocks receive a higher score, promoting reliable participation and network security."],["h4",{"id":"why-availability-matters"},"Why Availability Matters"],["p",{},"Without availability, a validator could have high dominance and reliability scores but still not actively contribute to the network. This would misrepresent their role in maintaining the network''s operation. The availability factor ensures that only validators selected to produce blocks are evaluated, penalizing those that fail to participate effectively, whether due to inactivity, being jailed, or missing blocks when offline."],["p",{},"In this context, we cannot measure how long a validator is online but can only determine when they are selected to produce blocks. A validator may be considered active yet fail to produce blocks. Availability focuses on participation in block production, regardless of online or offline status. For instance:"],["ul",{},["li",{},"A validator might not produce blocks because it hasn’t been selected in a specific period."],["li",{},"In the other hand, a validator may be offline, causing it to The account is controlled with a private key unique to the user. This type of account is similar to a bank account with an address, a balance, and a key. When a user sends or receives NIM, the corresponding balance is updated accordingly. A basic account is created when any user sends NIM to a non-existent address."],["h2",{"id":"htlc"},"HTLC"],["p",{},"An HTLC is a conditional payment implemented by a script in the blockchain. It is a smart contract that enables a party to transfer assets to another party without relying on a third party. The contract acts as the escrow party for both sender and recipient."],["table",{},["thead",{},["tr",{},["th",{"align":"left"},"Data Field"],["th",{"align":"left"},"Description"]]],["tbody",{},["tr",{},["td",{"align":"left"},["code",{},"balance"]],["td",{"align":"left"},"The amount of NIM at any given time. If the time elapses, the sender can withdraw the funds"]],["tr",{},["td",{"align":"left"},["code",{},"total_amount"]],["td",{"align":"left"},"The initial amount decided in the contract creation"]]]],["p",{},"Anyone can create an HTLC whose structural values are static (determined by the contract owner) except for the balance, which changes every time NIM is withdrawn. Also, once the HTLC is created, no NIM can be added to the contract."],["p",{},"There are three different transactions to unlock the funds, and each one results in a new balance on the HTLC:"],["ol",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Timeout resolve"],": After the timeout elapses, the sender can redeem the funds."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Regular transfer"],": The recipient can withdraw the funds entirely or partially before the ",["code",{},"timeout"]," elapses. If the ",["code",{},"hash_count"]," is three, the contract owner can present a hash that was rehashed two times resulting in the ",["code",{},"hash_root"]," and then withdraw two-thirds of the funds. The owner can also present the ",["code",{},"pre_image"]," and withdraw the ",["code",{},"total_amount"]," immediately."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Early resolve"],": When both sender and recipient sign the transaction, the funds can be withdrawn at any time."]],["h2",{"id":"vesting-contract"},"Vesting Contract"],["p",{},"The vesting contract allows a user to lock funds for a period of time and unlock them in a predefined timetable. INSERT INTO _content_docs VALUES ('docs/protocol/consensus/block-format.md', 'Block Format', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Micro Blocks"],": include user-generated transactions and are produced and signed by a validator according to the validator selection process. There are two types: election and checkpoint."]],["h2",{"id":"micro-blocks"},"Micro Blocks"],["p",{},"Micro blocks are the blocks for including transactions on the Nimiq blockchain. A validator, randomly selected through a ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/consensus/verifiable-random-functions"},"VRF-based process"]," which ensures randomness and decentralization, produces a new micro block. If a validator fails to produce a block, the remaining validators can agree on a skip block to maintain the blockchain''s continuity. The structure of a micro block is divided into three parts: ",["strong",{},"header"],", ",["strong",{},"body"],", and ",["strong",{},"justification"],"."],["h3",{"id":"micro-header"},"Micro Header"],["table",{},["thead",{},["tr",{},["th",{},["strong",{},"Field"]],["th",{},["strong",{},"Data Type"]],["th",{},["strong",{},"Description"]]]],["tbody",{},["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"network"]],["td",{},["code",{},"NetworkId"]],["td",{},"The network ID associated with the block, such as Mainnet or Testnet"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"version"]],["td",{},["code",{},"u16"]],["td",{},"The block''s version number. A change in the version number implies a hard fork. It can currently only be 1."]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"block_number"]],["td",{},["code",{},"u32"]],["td",{},"The number of the block, representing its height in the blockchain"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"timestamp"]],["td",{},["code",{},"u64"]],["td",{},"The block''s Unix creation timestamp in milliseconds, indicating when the block was produced"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"parent_hash"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Blake2bHash"]],["td",{},"The hash of the preceding block''s header (micro or macro). This ensures a direct link to its predecessor"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"seed"]],["td",{},["code",{},"VrfSeed"]],["td",{},"The output of the VxEdsa VRF function derived from the seed of the previous block, using the validator key of the block producer"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"extra_data"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Vec<u8>"]],["td",{},"Data that can be freely chosen by the producing validator, the default client leaves it empty"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"state_root"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Blake2bHash"]],["td",{},"The root of the Merkle tree representing the blockchain state, acting as a commitment to the current state"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"body_root"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Blake2sHash"]],["td",{},"The hash of the block''s body, serving as a commitment to its content"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"diff_root"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Blake2bHash"]],["td",{},"The root of the trie diff tree proof, authenticating the state changes between blocks"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"history_root"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Blake2bHash"]],["td",{},"The root of a Merkle Mountain Range covering all transactions in the current epoch up until the current block"]]]],["h3",{"id":"micro-body"},"Micro Body"],["table",{},["thead",{},["tr",{},["th",{},["strong",{},"Field"]],["th",{},["strong",{},"Data Type"]],["th",{},["strong",{},"Description"]]]],["tbody",{},["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"equivocation_proofs"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Vec<EquivocationProof>"]],["td",{},"A vector containing equivocation proofs for this block. This field may be empty if no such proofs exist or if the block producer chooses not to include any"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"transactions"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Vec<ExecutedTransaction>"]],["td",{},"A vector containing the transactions for this block. It may be empty if there are no transactions available for inclusion or if the block producer chooses not to include any"]]]],["h3",{"id":"micro-justification"},"Micro Justification"],["table",{},["thead",{},["tr",{},["th",{},["strong",{},"Field"]],["th",{},["strong",{},"Data Type"]],["th",{},["strong",{},"Description"]]]],["tbody",{},["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"Micro(Ed25519Signature)"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Ed25519Signature"]],["td",{},"The block producer''s signature. This is used as the justification when the block is produced within the expected time"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"Skip(SkipBlockProof)"]],["td",{},["code",{},"SkipBlockProof"]],["td",{},"Contains the aggregated BLS signatures of validators for a skip block. Used as justification when the block isn''t produced within the expected time"]]]],["p",{},"Only one of these fields is used at a time as justification, depending on whether the block is produced within the expected timeframe or not."],["h3",{"id":"skip-blocks"},"Skip Blocks"],["p",{},"When a micro block is not produced within the expected timeframe, the remaining elected validators step in and create a skip block in the expected micro block’s place. Unlike a regular micro block, a skip block does not include transactions and is agreed and signed by over two-thirds of the validators of the current epoch. This block replaces the micro block, thus ‘skipping’ past it. For detailed information, refer to the ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/validators/skip-blocks"},"skip blocks"]," documentation."],["h2",{"id":"macro-blocks"},"Macro Blocks"],["p",{},"There are two types of macro blocks: ",["strong",{},"election"]," and ",["strong",{},"checkpoint"],", each serving a specific role. Election macro blocks update the validator list, defining which validators will participate in the next epoch; these blocks also close epochs. Checkpoint macro blocks finalize transactions and close batches but do not change the validator list."],["p",{},"Macro blocks need consensus of 2/3 of the validator ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/validators/slots"},"slots"]," to be confirmed, ensuring finality and cementing the state of the blockchain at regular intervals. The structure of a macro block is divided into three parts: ",["strong",{},"header"],", ",["strong",{},"body"],", and ",["strong",{},"justification"],"."],["h3",{"id":"macro-header"},"Macro Header"],["table",{},["thead",{},["tr",{},["th",{},["strong",{},"Field"]],["th",{},["strong",{},"Data Type"]],["th",{},["strong",{},"Description"]]]],["tbody",{},["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"network"]],["td",{},["code",{},"NetworkId"]],["td",{},"The network ID associated with the block, such as Mainnet or Testnet"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"version"]],["td",{},["code",{},"u16"]],["td",{},"The block''s version number. Changing this implies a hard fork"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"block_number"]],["td",{},["code",{},"u32"]],["td",{},"The number of the block, representing its height in the blockchain"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"round"]],["td",{},["code",{},"u32"]],["td",{},"The specific Tendermint round in which this block was proposed"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"timestamp"]],["td",{},["code",{},"u64"]],["td",{},"The Unix creation timestamp (in milliseconds) indicating when the block was produced"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"parent_hash"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Blake2bHash"]],["td",{},"The hash of the preceding block''s header (can only be a micro block)"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"parent_election_hash"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Blake2bHash"]],["td",{},"The hash of the header from the previous election macro block"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"interlink"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Option<Vec<Blake2bHash>>"]],["td",{},"A vector of hashes linking to previous election blocks with epoch numbers ending in ",["em",{},"n"]," zeros in binary representation. This allows nodes to verify past blocks efficiently without needing to traverse the entire chain"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"seed"]],["td",{},["code",{},"VrfSeed"]],["td",{},"The output of the VxEdsa VRF function derived from the seed of the previous block, using the validator key of the block producer"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"extra_data"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Vec<u8>"]],["td",{},"Data that can be freely chosen by the producing validator, the default client leaves it empty"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"state_root"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Blake2bHash"]],["td",{},"The Merkle root representing the blockchain state, acting as a commitment to the current state"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"body_root"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Blake2sHash"]],["td",{},"The hash of the block body, serving as a commitment to its content"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"diff_root"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Blake2bHash"]],["td",{},"The root of the trie diff tree proof, which authenticates the state transitions or changes between blocks"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"history_root"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Blake2bHash"]],["td",{},"The root of a Merkle Mountain Range covering all transactions that occurred in the current epoch up until this block"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"validators"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Option<Validators>"]],["td",{},"Information about the upcoming validator list. Present only in election macro blocks. The ",["code",{},"Validators"]," struct contains a list of validators ordered by their slots and a mapping of validator addresses to their slot range."]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"next_batch_initial_punished_set"]],["td",{},["code",{},"BitSet"]],["td",{},"A bitset representing validator slots that are banned from producing blocks in the next batch due to misbehavior"]]]],["h3",{"id":"macro-body"},"Macro Body"],["table",{},["thead",{},["tr",{},["th",{},["strong",{},"Field"]],["th",{},["strong",{},"Data Type"]],["th",{},["strong",{},"Description"]]]],["tbody",{},["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"transactions"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Vec<RewardTransaction>"]],["td",{},"Contains the reward transactions of this block, distributing block rewards and transaction fees of the current batch to validators. Macro blocks do not include user-generated transactions."]]]],["h3",{"id":"macro-justification"},"Macro Justification"],["table",{},["thead",{},["tr",{},["th",{},["strong",{},"Field"]],["th",{},["strong",{},"Data Type"]],["th",{},["strong",{},"Description"]]]],["tbody",{},["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"round"]],["td",{},["code",{},"u32"]],["td",{},"The specific tendermint round in which the block was accepted. This is used to verify that the signature corresponds to the correct round"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"sig"]],["td",{},["code",{},"MultiSignature"]],["td",{},"The aggregated BLS signature of the validators’ precommit votes for the block, confirming validator consensus"]]]],["h2",{"id":"relation-between-micro-and-macro-blocks"},"Relation Between Micro and Macro Blocks"],["p",{},"Micro and macro blocks are interconnected. This connection ensures blockchain continuity and finality. The following section focuses on how these block types interact."],["p",{},["strong",{},"Block Connections"],"\nAll blocks are sequentially linked by the parent hash, forming a continuous chain, and every checkpoint macro block points to its election macro block by the parent election hash. The following diagram illustrates the connection between micro and macro blocks. Each block is directly connected to its predecessor through the parent hash."],["img",{"className":["object-contain","max-h-[max(80vh,220px)]"],"src":"/assets/images/protocol/macro-micro.png","alt":"macro and micro block connection"}],["p",{},["em",{},"Note: This diagram is a simplified representation. The actual blockchain includes a fixed number of blocks per batch and epoch, which this diagram does not reflect."]],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Parent Hash"],": Links each block to its direct predecessor."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Election Parent Hash"],": Connects checkpoint macro blocks to their preceding election macro block."]],["h2",{"id":"blockchain-format"},"Blockchain Format"],["p",{},"The Nimiq blockchain is structured into several subsets of blocks called epochs and batches."],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Batches"],": A batch consists of 59 ",["strong",{},"micro blocks"]," produced one after another. Each batch ends with a ",["strong",{},"checkpoint"]," macro block, which finalizes the transactions included in the preceding micro blocks. Since the micro blocks are supposed to be spaced 1 second apart, a batch is expected to take 1 minute."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Epochs"],": An epoch is a set of 1440 batches. Each epoch ends with an ",["strong",{},"election"]," macro block, which not only finalizes the transactions in the preceding batch but also updates the validator list of the entire epoch. Since a batch is expected to take about 1 minute, 1440 batches are expected to take 12 hours."]],["img",{"className":["object-contain","max-h-[max(300px)]"],"src":"/assets/images/protocol/block-struct-3.png","alt":"blockchain structure"}],["h2",{"id":"block-finality"},"Block Finality"],["p",{},"Block finality in the Nimiq PoS blockchain ensures that all transactions within the finalized blocks are permanent and cannot be reversed. This is achieved through a balance of micro block production and periodic macro block consensus. While micro blocks add transactions to the chain, these transactions only reach finality when confirmed by a macro block. Macro blocks, produced at regular intervals using Tendermint consensus, finalize the state of all preceding micro blocks in a batch."]],"toc":{"title":"","searchDepth":2,"depth":2,"links":[{"id":"micro-blocks","depth":2,"text":"Micro Blocks","children":[{"id":"micro-header","depth":3,"text":"Micro Header"},{"id":"micro-body","depth":3,"text":"Micro Body"},{"id":"micro-justification","depth":3,"text":"Micro Justification"},{"id":"skip-blocks","depth":3,"text":"Skip Blocks"}]},{"id":"macro-blocks","depth":2,"text":"Macro Blocks","children":[{"id":"macro-header","depth":3,"text":"Macro Header"},{"id":"macro-body","depth":3,"text":"Macro Body"},{"id":"macro-justification","depth":3,"text":"Macro Justification"}]},{"id":"relation-between-micro-and-macro-blocks","depth":2,"text":"Relation Between Micro and Macro Blocks"},{"id":"blockchain-format","depth":2,"text":"Blockchain Format"},{"id":"block-finality","depth":2,"text":"Block Finality"}]}}', 'In the Nimiq blockchain, blocks are categorized into microblocks and macroblocks. INSERT INTO _content_docs VALUES ('docs/protocol/consensus/equivocation-proofs.md', 'Equivocation Proofs', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["p",{},"An equivocation proof proves that a validator misbehaved. The offending validator is automatically jailed as soon as the equivocation proof is added to the blockchain. Validators enter an 8-epoch jail, lose their rewards, and the ability to produce blocks during the jail period. Honest validators refrain from any misbehavior, as it is not in their interest to lose rewards or be restrained from block production."],["p",{},"Validators can submit proofs attesting to malicious behavior. While submitting these proofs is considered good practice, it is optional for validators. They can submit any proof from the moment they witness an offense up to the last micro block at the end of the next epoch. The reporting window, spanning almost two epochs, provides sufficient time for validators to identify, address, and submit proofs of malicious behavior to be included in the blockchain."],["p",{},"Validators can misbehave in three ways:"],["ul",{},["li",{},"Fork the chain"],["li",{},"Proposing different macro blocks in the same round"],["li",{},"Voting twice in the same round and step"]],["h3",{"id":"fork-proofs"},"Fork Proofs"],["p",{},"A fork occurs when a malicious validator generates two micro blocks at the same block height, aiming to create a chain split or attempt double spending."],["p",{},"The fork concludes once an honest validator is chosen as the subsequent block producer. Block production then resumes according to the consensus (one micro block per block height), and any validator can submit a fork proof attesting to the malicious behavior of the previous validator."],["p",{},"Forks may also end if the next block is a macro block. Although a malicious validator may attempt to propose 2 blocks at the same block height (refer to the double proposal proof below), the consensus requirement dictates that more than 2/3 of validators must agree on a block proposal before it is added to the blockchain; thus, there is no way to continue a fork."],["code-group",{},["pre",{"className":"language-rust shiki shiki-themes material-theme-lighter material-theme material-theme-palenight","code":"pub struct ForkProof {\n    validator_address: Address,\n    header1: MicroHeader,\n    header2: MicroHeader,\n    justification1: SchnorrSignature,\n    justification2: SchnorrSignature,\n}\n","language":"rust","meta":"","style":""},["code",{"__ignoreMap":""},["span",{"class":"line","line":1},["span",{},"pub struct ForkProof {\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":2},["span",{},"    validator_address: Address,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":3},["span",{},"    header1: MicroHeader,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":4},["span",{},"    header2: MicroHeader,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":5},["span",{},"    justification1: SchnorrSignature,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":6},["span",{},"    justification2: SchnorrSignature,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":7},["span",{},"}\n"]]]]],["p",{},"The offending validator address, two micro headers from the same block height and the respective signatures are enough to prove the malicious behavior."],["h3",{"id":"double-proposal-proofs"},"Double Proposal Proofs"],["p",{},"Macro blocks are produced using the Tendermint algorithm. A validator, selected as the round leader, proposes a macro block and gossips its proposal. Malicious validators may attempt to propose two different macro headers at the same block height, round and step."],["p",{},"Honest validators vote for one proposal per round; therefore, receiving more than one proposal from the same validator for the same block indicates misbehavior from a malicious validator. Double proposal proofs serve to identify and punish a validator if it attempts to sign conflicting blocks, demonstrating malicious behavior."],["code-group",{},["pre",{"className":"language-rust shiki shiki-themes material-theme-lighter material-theme material-theme-palenight","code":"pub struct DoubleProposalProof {\n    validator_address: Address,\n    header1: MacroHeader,\n    header2: MacroHeader,\n    justification1: SchnorrSignature,\n    justification2: SchnorrSignature,\n}\n","language":"rust","meta":"","style":""},["code",{"__ignoreMap":""},["span",{"class":"line","line":1},["span",{},"pub struct DoubleProposalProof {\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":2},["span",{},"    validator_address: Address,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":3},["span",{},"    header1: MacroHeader,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":4},["span",{},"    header2: MacroHeader,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":5},["span",{},"    justification1: SchnorrSignature,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":6},["span",{},"    justification2: SchnorrSignature,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":7},["span",{},"}\n"]]]]],["p",{},"The offending validator address, two macro headers from the same round and the respective signatures are enough to prove the malicious behavior."],["h3",{"id":"double-vote-proofs"},"Double Vote Proofs"],["p",{},"Validators are expected to vote block or ",["em",{},"nil"]," for a single Tendermint proposal per round and step. Voting for different proposals at the same block height, round, and step is considered a double vote, disrupting Tendermint''s voting principle. The double vote proof is used to identify and punish validators for their misbehavior."],["code-group",{},["pre",{"className":"language-rust shiki shiki-themes material-theme-lighter material-theme material-theme-palenight","code":"pub struct DoubleVoteProof {\n    validator_address: Address,\n    tendermint_id: TendermintIdentifier,\n    proposal_hash1: Option<Blake2sHash>,\n    proposal_hash2: Option<Blake2sHash>,\n    signature1: AggregateSignature,\n    signature2: AggregateSignature,\n    signers1: BitSet,\n    signers2: BitSet,\n}\n","language":"rust","meta":"","style":""},["code",{"__ignoreMap":""},["span",{"class":"line","line":1},["span",{},"pub struct DoubleVoteProof {\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":2},["span",{},"    validator_address: Address,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":3},["span",{},"    tendermint_id: TendermintIdentifier,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":4},["span",{},"    proposal_hash1: Option<Blake2sHash>,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":5},["span",{},"    proposal_hash2: Option<Blake2sHash>,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":6},["span",{},"    signature1: AggregateSignature,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":7},["span",{},"    signature2: AggregateSignature,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":8},["span",{},"    signers1: BitSet,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":9},["span",{},"    signers2: BitSet,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":10},["span",{},"}\n"]]]]],["p",{},"The proof serves to identify the malicious validator, pointing the block height, round, and step at which the double voting occurred, along with the two proposal hashes. Validators combine their signatures into a single aggregate signature. However, aggregate signatures cannot be verified without knowing who voted. To verify the signature, the bitset of signers is included. This bitset identifies the validators who participated in signing the specific proposals, thus also pinpointing and confirming instances of double voting by a malicious validator."],["style",{},"html
