INSERT INTO _content_docs VALUES ('docs/protocol/consensus/punishments.md', 'Punishments', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["p",{},"The blockchain deals with misbehavior based on the nature of the offense:"],["ul",{},["li",{},"A delay in block production constitutes a minor offense. In such cases, the associated slot is deactivated, and the rewards are burned."],["li",{},"For more severe offenses like forking, double voting, or double proposals, the validator is jailed. All validator slots are deactivated for a set period, and rewards for these slots are burned."]],["callout",{"color":"info","icon":"i-tabler-info-circle"},"Burned rewards are sent to the burned address: ",["code",{},"NQ07 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000"],". Note that no one can or will use the funds sent to this address."],["h2",{"id":"block-production-delay"},"Block production delay"],["p",{},"A delay in block production is categorized as a minor offense. Such delays can occur due to intentional or unintentional circumstances, such as unexpected internet connectivity issues. Determining the intent behind the delay can be challenging."],["p",{},"Validators hold multiple slots and produce micro blocks with one slot at a time. As a consequence of delaying the block production, rewards for the corresponding slot are burned. The slot is added to the ",["code",{},"punished_slots"]," set, which remains for the current batch and the next one, to account for reward distribution at the next batch. This set ensures accurate reward distribution during the upcoming batch."],["p",{},"The misbehaving slot gets deactivated but can reactivate itself one block after committing the offense. While delaying block production is not considered a severe misbehavior due to replacing the missing block with a skip block, the rewards for the misbehaving slot are burned regardless as a penalty."],["h2",{"id":"jail"},"Jail"],["p",{},"When a validator acts maliciously on purpose, it gets jailed. Getting jailed can happen for a variety of malicious behaviors, including:"],["ul",{},["li",{},"Creating a fork or continuing to produce on a fork"],["li",{},"Making a double proposal on Tendermint"],["li",{},"Casting more than one vote per slot on Tendermint proposals"]],["p",{},"Any rational validator that witnesses one of these behaviors can report it by including a proof in a micro block. Once the proof that attests to the misbehavior is submitted, the validator is immediately jailed."],["p",{},"As these are more severe offenses that interfere with the blockchain, the consequences are also more severe. When a validator is jailed:"],["ul",{},["li",{},"It is immediately removed from the ",["code",{},"active_validators"]," set, and all its slots are marked as punished in the ",["code",{},"punished_slots"]," set in the staking contract."],["li",{},"It gets locked for 8 epochs. However, it is required to continue block production until the end of the current batch and vote for Tendermint blocks until the end of the epoch; from thereafter, it is not considered for block production until the locking period ends. The withdrawal lock takes effect immediately upon getting jailed."],["li",{},"It loses its rewards for the jail period."]],["p",{},"The ",["code",{},"active_validators"]," and ",["code",{},"punished_slots"]," sets are updated at every block in the ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/validators/staking-contract"},"staking contract"],". However, if a validator shifts from active to inactive or jailed, it is required to produce blocks until the current batch concludes, as it remains included in the validator slot list for that batch. However, it is no longer considered to produce blocks for further batches starting at the next checkpoint block."],["p",{},"In the context of Tendermint votes, a validator that shifts to inactive or jailed mid-epoch must vote until the end of the epoch. This is because there are no mid-epoch elections to replace the slots of the inactive or jailed validator. Thus, it must participate in the entire epoch''s voting process to maintain the necessary validator count for consensus."],["p",{},"Once the validator is out of jail, it moves to the inactive state. Validators with the ",["code",{},"automatic_reactivate"]," feature set to ",["code",{},"true"]," within their configuration will reactivate upon release. Furthermore, even if the validator activates itself right after being released, it will only be considered again to participate in the consensus at the next epoch upon election."],["p",{},"If a validator wants to withdraw its deposit after being released, it can do so immediately if it does not reactivate. If the validator becomes active again, it must be inactive for a predetermined time to account for possible new misbehaviors."],["h3",{"id":"reporting-window"},"Reporting window"],["p",{},"There is a reporting window for when validators can submit such proofs. From the block after the offense up to the end of the epoch after the next election block. The reporting window corresponds to the period a validator must be in the inactive state to be able to delete its account and withdraw its funds."],["h3",{"id":"impact-on-stakers"},"Impact on stakers"],["p",{},"Punishments also affect the validator''s stakers, as the locking period for the staker''s stake aligns with the validator''s locking period. Stakers can either maintain their stake with their validator or remove it once the jail period ends if their stake remains inactive."]],"toc":{"title":"","searchDepth":2,"depth":2,"links":[{"id":"block-production-delay","depth":2,"text":"Block production delay"},{"id":"jail","depth":2,"text":"Jail","children":[{"id":"reporting-window","depth":3,"text":"Reporting window"},{"id":"impact-on-stakers","depth":3,"text":"Impact on stakers"}]}]}}', 'Validators are responsible for ensuring the security and stability of a blockchain network. They are rewarded for participating and punished if they fail to contribute according to the consensus. Misbehaving always results in either losing the rewards or going to jail, where the validator gets locked up for a period and loses the rewards. The types of punishment are divided based on the severity of the misbehavior.', 'md', 'i-tabler:alert-triangle', NULL, '{"matter":{"title":"Punishments","description":"Validators are responsible for ensuring the security and stability of a blockchain network. INSERT INTO _content_docs VALUES ('docs/protocol/consensus/verifiable-random-functions.md', 'Verifiable Random Functions', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Prove function"],": Given a message and a user''s private key as input to the function, a proof is generated."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Verify function"],": By running this function, anyone can verify the proof''s correctness. The corresponding public key, the proof, and the previous message are taken as input to verify if the proof was correctly computed."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Extract function"],": The function takes as input the proof and outputs a random value. This random value is the entropy that was extracted from the proof. Thus, the entropy randomly takes a piece of the proof and generates a random value."]],["p",{},"VRFs have three security properties:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Pseudorandomness"],": Despite the output appearing to be random, it is generated with deterministic computation. The same input always results in the same output."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Uniqueness"],": Only one output is possible for each input and private key."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Public verifiability"],": Anyone that holds the corresponding public key can verify the correctness of the output."]],["h2",{"id":"vrf-implementation"},"VRF implementation"],["p",{},"Elected validators produce random seeds implementing VRFs with the ",["a",{"href":"https://www.signal.org/docs/specifications/xeddsa/#vxeddsa","rel":["nofollow"]},"VXEdDSA"]," algorithm. Both micro block producers and macro block proposers generate random seeds. These are stored in the header of every block."],["p",{},"The elected validator selected to produce a random seed takes as input the entropy from the previous random seed as the message of the proof function along with its own private key. This outputs a proof that is the new random seed. Then, any node can run the verify function with the corresponding public key to check the proof''s correctness. Lastly, any node can run the extract function on the random seed to extract its entropy, which is used to generate a random value."],["p",{},"A random value is generated by extracting the entropy from the proof. The random seed cannot be used directly since it is a controllable value that the elected validator could change. Extracting the entropy from the proof is essential as it ensures security and prevents any node from controlling this value."],["p",{},"The prove and extract functions allow a chain of seeds since generating a new random seed occurs using data from the previous random seed. Every random seed is intrinsically related to the previous one."],["p",{},"Random seeds are used in three cases:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Validator slot selection:"]," A new ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/validators/slots"},"validator slot list"]," is selected at the end of each epoch. The entropy extracted from the random seed is used to select this new list. The block''s proposer takes the active validator set along with the entropy and creates the list."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Slot owner selection:"]," A new slot owner list is created at every block. The block''s producer extracts the entropy from the random seed to shuffle the validator slot list resulting in the view slot list for the block."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Rewards distribution:"]," Elected validators are rewarded at the end of every batch. The rewards are distributed among the elected validators. In every batch, the validator slot list has 512 validator slots. Due to the potential inability to evenly divide the batch reward among 512 slots, entropy from the random seed is utilized to determine which elected validator receives the remainder."]]],"toc":{"title":"","searchDepth":2,"depth":2,"links":[{"id":"vrf-implementation","depth":2,"text":"VRF implementation"}]}}', 'A verifiable random function is a pseudo-random function that generates a random value and allows anyone to verify if it was correctly calculated. It combines a proving function, a verifying function, and an extracting function. A key pair is used to operate these functions.', 'md', 'i-tabler:dice', NULL, '{"matter":{"title":"Verifiable Random Functions","description":"A verifiable random function is a pseudo-random function that generates a random value and allows anyone to verify if it was correctly calculated. It combines a proving function, a verifying function, and an extracting function. A key pair is used to operate these functions."}}', '{"title":"Verifiable Random Functions","order":2}', '/protocol/consensus/verifiable-random-functions', '{"title":"Verifiable Random Functions","description":"A verifiable random function is a pseudo-random function that generates a random value and allows anyone to verify if it was correctly calculated. It combines a proving function, a verifying function, and an extracting function. A key pair is used to operate these functions."}', 'protocol/consensus/verifiable-random-functions', '9WGLs_oTgi9Q2pgFh8iMPf3SWniK_P8AowJNCu_y4RM'); -- 9WGLs_oTgi9Q2pgFh8iMPf3SWniK_P8AowJNCu_y4RM
INSERT INTO _content_docs VALUES ('docs/protocol/economics/rewards.md', 'Rewards', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["p",{},"Validators receive rewards under these conditions:"],["ul",{},["li",{},"Producing a micro block on time"],["li",{},"Avoiding creating or building on forks"],["li",{},"Voting once per slot for each Tendermint proposal"],["li",{},"Broadcasting Tendermint proposals on time"]],["p",{},"Validators receive rewards proportional to their total stake. Validators with a higher total stake earn higher rewards. Users who cannot become validators can delegate their NIM to a validator, increasing the validator''s total stake and potential rewards. While validators receive their rewards on-chain, they distribute rewards to stakers off-chain. Validators handle the distribution of these rewards according to their arrangements with their stakers."],["h3",{"id":"reward-distribution"},"Reward distribution"],["p",{},"Validators receive rewards to their reward address every batch. However, the distribution of rewards for a batch occurs at the end of the following batch. This delay is necessary to prevent malicious validators from attempting an offense in the last block of a batch. With this delay, there is sufficient time to submit an equivocation proof on the malicious validator."],["p",{},"For minor offenses, such as delaying block production, validators lose the rewards for the affected slot. For severe misbehavior, like equivocations, the rewards for all slots are burned. This also affects stakers, as the validator does not receive rewards for at least 8 epochs if jailed, regardless of any arrangements made with their stakers."],["p",{},"The reward distribution is proportional to the total stake and is evenly distributed per slot. This means a validator with 15 slots receives fewer rewards than one with 50 slots. Also, if a validator with 15 slots delays block production, it will only receive rewards for 14 of its 15 slots, but if it attempts to broadcast a double proposal, it does not receive any rewards for at least 8 epochs."],["h3",{"id":"reward-calculation"},"Reward calculation"],["p",{},"The rewards comprise the coinbase and transaction fees. The coinbase represents the coins printed at the end of each batch, while transaction fees encompass the total fees from transactions within the batch."],["p",{},"In Albatross, both the coinbase and transaction fees fluctuate. In contrast to Bitcoin''s fixed coinbase, which decreases by around 50% every four years, our coinbase varies over time as new coins are printed per batch rather than per block."],["p",{},"To INSERT INTO _content_docs VALUES ('docs/protocol/glossary.md', 'Glossary Nimiq PoS', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["h2",{"id":"account"},"Account"],["h2",{"id":"address"},"Address"],["p",{},"The identifier of an account."],["h2",{"id":"aggregated-signature"},"Aggregated Signature"],["p",{},"The outcome of calculating multiple signatures into a single signature. Aggregating signatures reduces the amount of data that several single signatures hold. Signature aggregation is used for signing ",["a",{"href":"#skip-block"},"skip blocks"]," and aggregating votes for ",["a",{"href":"#tendermint"},"Tendermint"]," voting."],["h2",{"id":"albatross"},"Albatross"],["p",{},"Nimiq Proof-of-Stake algorithm. It is an algorithm inspired by the BFT protocol, assuming that 3",["em",{},"f"],"+1 validators, at maximum ",["em",{},"f"],", are malicious. Nimiq PoS protocol achieves probabilistic finality by combining ",["a",{"href":"#micro-block"},"micro blocks"]," and ",["a",{"href":"#macro-block"},"macro blocks"],". Micro blocks are produced and signed by a single validator, while macro blocks mark the end of an epoch and are produced using Tendermint, which ensures finality as they are agreed upon by at least 2f+1 validators."],["h2",{"id":"bls-signature"},"BLS Signature"],["p",{},"The BLS signature scheme uses bilinear pairing and elliptic curve cryptography and has many valuable features. In Nimiq PoS, BLS is used for signature aggregation, allowing ",["em",{},"n"]," signatures to be combined into a single signature, significantly decreasing the required data. Validators generate a public and private key, and each validator uses their private key to produce a signature. BLS signatures allow for efficient signature aggregation, resulting in substantial space savings."],["h2",{"id":"batch"},"Batch"],["p",{},"The interval between two macro blocks, ",["a",{"href":"#election-block"},"election"]," or ",["a",{"href":"#checkpoint-block"},"checkpoint"]," macro blocks. A batch consists of several micro blocks, closing on a macro block."],["h2",{"id":"block"},"Block"],["p",{},"A block contains a set of transactions and data attesting to the transactions'' validity. Our blockchain holds 2 types of blocks: micro blocks and macro blocks. Validators are responsible for gathering and adding data to a block based on consensus rules. The block''s validity is determined by the data contained within it, and it is added to the blockchain once the network verifies it."],["h2",{"id":"block-number"},"Block Number"],["p",{},"Block number and block height can be used interchangeably. The block number refers to the block''s position relative to the blockchain, starting at block 0 - the ",["a",{"href":"#genesis-block"},"genesis block"],". The block number of 9 is the ninth block after the genesis block."],["h2",{"id":"body"},"Body"],["p",{},"Part of the structure of a block. The body contains the block''s transactions and fork proofs in a micro block, but it can be empty if no transactions or ",["a",{"href":"#equivocation-proof"},"equivocation proofs"]," have occurred. In a macro block, the body stores rewards related data."],["h2",{"id":"checkpoint-block"},"Checkpoint Block"],["p",{},"A type of macro block that marks the end of a batch. Checkpoint blocks maintain the same list of validators that was selected in the previous election macro block and serve as a finality point for the blockchain state between epochs."],["h2",{"id":"coinbase"},"Coinbase"],["p",{},"Given our supply formula, the coinbase is the number of new coins printed at the end of a batch."],["h2",{"id":"commitment"},"Commitment"],["p",{},"A commitment is a cryptographic primitive that enables a node to commit to a value without revealing it, resulting in less data to the endpoint. The value on which the node committed remains private, but its accuracy can be proven without revealing the value itself. The value is part of the proof, and nodes can verify that the value matches the commitment. In our consensus algorithm, the blockchain uses commitments in three areas: commitments to specific parts of the blockchain, such as transactions in a block and accounts in the current state, state commitments as input to the ",["a",{"href":"#zero-knowledge-proof-zkp"},"zero-knowledge proofs"]," in the light sync, and commitments to secret values in our multi-sig scheme. Ultimately, using commitments enables data compaction by using hashes and Merkle trees. For instance, it allows light clients to sync without downloading the entire blockchain while preserving the integrity of the blockchain."],["h2",{"id":"compressed-signature"},"Compressed Signature"],["p",{},"A compressed signature is a compacted version of a typical digital signature generated from a full signature. The compressed signature provides the same level of security as a full signature while decreasing the storage space of one."],["h2",{"id":"consensus-algorithm"},"Consensus Algorithm"],["p",{},"A type of algorithm used to reach an agreement between nodes in a shared state. Nimiq PoS uses the Albatross consensus algorithm, which combines micro blocks for fast transaction processing with macro blocks for finality. Validators work together to follow the consensus rules, ensuring network security and maintaining a consistent blockchain state."],["h2",{"id":"deposit"},"Deposit"],["p",{},"The initial amount of NIM that a validator must lock in the staking contract to become eligible for validator selection, which is 100''000 NIM. This is different from the stake amount and serves as a security deposit."],["h2",{"id":"double-proposal"},"Double Proposal"],["p",{},"The act of submitting two different Tendermint proposals in the same round."],["h2",{"id":"double-vote"},"Double Vote"],["p",{},"The act of voting twice for the same block height, at the same round and step of Tendermint."],["h2",{"id":"election-block"},"Election Block"],["p",{},"A type of macro block that marks the end of an epoch. Election blocks are where a new validator list is selected from the potential validator set and integrated into the staking contract for the upcoming epoch."],["h2",{"id":"entropy"},"Entropy"],["p",{},"The measure of unpredictability of a random value. Using the entropy of the random seed present in a block, the process involves hashing and generating a new random seed. The more random the value, the higher the entropy. Thus, by using the entropy of a random seed, a random, unpredictable, and secure value can be produced."],["h2",{"id":"epoch"},"Epoch"],["p",{},"The time between two election macro blocks marks an epoch. An epoch starts with a micro block after an election macro block and ends at an election macro block, including all the micro blocks and checkpoint macro blocks in between."],["h2",{"id":"equivocation"},"Equivocation"],["p",{},"An equivocation is when a validator acts maliciously against the consensus protocol. This can involve creating two blocks at the same height, proposing two Tendermint blocks for the same block height, or even voting twice for a proposal during the same round and step."],["h2",{"id":"equivocation-proof"},"Equivocation Proof"],["p",{},"An equivocation proof provides evidence of a validator''s misbehavior, leading to ",["a",{"href":"#jail"},"jail"]," upon proof submission by any honest validator."],["h2",{"id":"fork"},"Fork"],["p",{},"A split in the blockchain produced by a malicious validator. A malicious validator can fork the chain by producing two blocks at the same block height, attempting for a double-spend attack."],["h2",{"id":"fork-proof"},"Fork Proof"],["p",{},"Evidence that a validator has attempted to create a fork by producing multiple blocks at the same height. This proof is used to punish malicious validators."],["h2",{"id":"genesis-block"},"Genesis Block"],["p",{},"The designation of the first block in the blockchain - also known as block 0. Developers code it, and validators produce the following blocks. The genesis block determines the initial state of the blockchain, and it''s hardcoded by developers. Validators produce the subsequent blocks following it according to the rules hardcoded in the genesis block."],["h2",{"id":"header"},"Header"],["p",{},"Part of the structure of a block. The block''s header contains general data about the block, such as the block number, the timestamp, and the version. Headers include required data to the consensus and commitments to the block. It also connects the current block to the previous one."],["h2",{"id":"inherent"},"Inherent"],["p",{},"An inherent is a type of data that is intrinsic to the block and automatically generated by the protocol rather than submitted by users. Unlike transactions, inherents are applied at specific times and serve different purposes, depending on their type. Examples include finalizing an epoch, validator set changes, and other protocol-specific data that helps maintain the blockchain''s state and consensus rules."],["h2",{"id":"jail"},"Jail"],["p",{},"Going to jail refers to the validator''s state characterized by incurring severe misbehavior for actions such as forking or continuing on a fork. When a validator is in a jailed state, it is locked for 8 epochs and cannot be re-elected during this lockup. Additionally, all the validator''s rewards are burned."],["h2",{"id":"justification"},"Justification"],["p",{},"Part of the structure of a block. In micro blocks, the justification contains the information and signature of the validator who produced the block. Whereas in macro blocks, the justification consists of a round of signatures produced through the Tendermint protocol."],["h2",{"id":"leader"},"Leader"],["p",{},"The proposer selected to make a Tendermint proposal."],["h2",{"id":"luna"},"Luna"],["p",{},"The smallest unit of NIM."],["h2",{"id":"macro-block"},"Macro Block"],["p",{},"There are two types of macro blocks: checkpoint macro blocks and election macro blocks. A checkpoint macro block marks the end of a batch, while an election macro block marks the end of an epoch, where validators are chosen from the validator set. Macro blocks are produced using Tendermint and provide finality as they are build upon the agreement of at least 2f+1 validators."],["h2",{"id":"malicious-validator"},"Malicious Validator"],["p",{},"A validator that intentionally misbehaves, attempting to interfere with the standard consensus of the blockchain."],["h2",{"id":"mempool"},"Mempool"],["p",{},"A database where transactions are kept on hold until a validator includes them in a block. Once a validator includes it in a block, a transaction is considered verified and valid."],["h2",{"id":"merkle-tree"},"Merkle Tree"],["p",{},"A tree data structure where each leaf node is a hash of a data block and each non-leaf node is a hash of its children. Merkle trees are used in Nimiq to efficiently verify data integrity and provide compact proofs that specific data is included in a larger dataset. Nimiq uses ",["a",{"href":"storage/merkle-trees#merkle-mountain-range"},"Merkle Mountain Ranges"]," (MMRs) for transaction history and ",["a",{"href":"storage/merkle-trees"},"Merkle Radix"]," tries for account state management."],["h2",{"id":"micro-block"},"Micro Block"],["p",{},"A type of block produced by one validator at a time. Micro blocks include transactions but may also include equivocation proofs and skip block proofs."],["h2",{"id":"nim"},"NIM"],["p",{},"Nimiq''s native cryptocurrency."],["h2",{"id":"potential-validator"},"Potential Validator"],["p",{},"A validator who has staked coins to participate in the consensus but has not yet been included in the block production."],["h2",{"id":"proof-of-stake"},"Proof-of-Stake"],["p",{},"A blockchain model where nodes put their tokens as a deposit and get allowed to validate transactions in the blockchain. Nodes are elected proportionally by their stake. A higher deposit increases the probability of a node being selected as a validator."],["h2",{"id":"random-seed"},"Random Seed"],["p",{},"A seed is present in every block. Random seeds are used in the validator selection to create a random and secure value. The initial random seed is generated at the genesis block, and the subsequent random seeds are generated by implementing the VXEDdSA algorithm instantiated as a verifiable random function."],["h2",{"id":"recursive-zkp"},"Recursive ZKP"],["p",{},"A zero-knowledge proof that can prove the validity of other zero-knowledge proofs. This allows for efficient verification of long chains of blocks by compressing multiple proofs into a single proof. More on ",["a",{"href":"zkp/ZKP-and-recursive-SNARKs"},"ZKP and Recursive SNARKs"],"."],["h2",{"id":"signature"},"Signature"],["p",{},"A digital signature that authenticates a message by comprising three functions: generate, sign and verify. A node''s private key and a particular message generate a signature. The signature is then verified, given the signature and the node''s public key."],["h2",{"id":"slot"},"Slot"],["p",{},"A validator receives ",["em",{},"x"]," slots when selected to participate in the consensus, distributed in a range. Validators use their slots to produce, propose and sign blocks. The number of slots a validator gets depends on the amount of stake they have deposited in the staking contract."],["h2",{"id":"slot-owner"},"Slot Owner"],["p",{},"The slot selected to produce the current micro block."],["h2",{"id":"slot-range"},"Slot Range"],["p",{},"The continuous range of slot numbers assigned to a validator. Validators receive slots in sequential ranges, and the size of the range corresponds to their stake amount."],["h2",{"id":"skip-block"},"Skip Block"],["p",{},"A type of micro block that doesn''t include the standard micro block data (doesn''t contain transactions) and that is produced in place of a micro block when the slot owner delays the production of the block. Any validator from the validator list can add this block to the chain, which must include a skip block proof."],["h2",{"id":"skip-block-proof"},"Skip Block Proof"],["p",{},"Evidence that a skip block is legitimate and was produced due to a validator''s failure to produce a block on time. This proof is required to validate skip blocks and must contain signatures from at least 2",["em",{},"f"],"+1 validators to be considered valid."],["h2",{"id":"staker"},"Staker"],["p",{},"A node that doesn''t have the time or resources to be a validator, or chooses not to, can delegate its coins to a validator. Validators then validate blocks on behalf of the staker by using the staker''s coins as joined collateral."],["h2",{"id":"staking"},"Staking"],["p",{},"To lock a deposit in the staking contract. Either a validator stakes its deposit to the staking contract, or a staker does."],["h2",{"id":"staking-contract"},"Staking Contract"],["p",{},"A special contract that deals with functions regarding validators, stakers, and staking. The staking contract gathers all the contract data in a single location, keeping track of the validators'' balance, validators'' list, punishments, and rewards."],["h2",{"id":"supermajority"},"Supermajority"],["p",{},"Supermajority means that at least 2/3 of the total validator voting power must agree on a decision for it to be considered final and valid."],["h2",{"id":"supply"},"Supply"],["p",{},"Based on the supply formula, the supply is the number of coins in the blockchain at any given moment. The blockchain has a maximum supply of 21 Billion NIM."],["h2",{"id":"tendermint"},"Tendermint"],["p",{},"Tendermint is a Byzantine Fault Tolerant (BFT) consensus algorithm used to produce macro blocks. It operates in rounds where validators are selected as proposers based on VRF-based slot assignment. Each round consists of three phases: Propose, Prevote, and Precommit. Validators must reach 2",["em",{},"f"],"+1 consensus (",["a",{"href":"#supermajority"},"supermajority"],") at each phase. If consensus isn''t reached, the round times out and a new round begins with a new proposer. The algorithm includes a locking mechanism to ensure safety and liveness even with network partitions."],["h2",{"id":"transaction"},"Transaction"],["p",{},"A data structure that represents an activity or request recorded in the blockchain that alters its state. Most transactions transfer NIM from a sender to a recipient, which can be another account, a validator, or a smart contract. Transactions are user-generated included in micro blocks."],["h2",{"id":"transaction-fees"},"Transaction Fees"],["p",{},"A small fee charged in transactions. The transaction fees of a batch are calculated at the end of the batch and divided among the validators as part of the rewards."],["h2",{"id":"validator"},"Validator"],["p",{},"An active validator is a node that has been selected to participate in the consensus for each epoch based on the stake deposited in the staking contract. The higher the stake, the greater the likelihood of being selected as a validator for the upcoming epoch. Once selected, validators can produce micro blocks, participate in macro block consensus, and receive rewards for their efforts."],["h2",{"id":"validator-list"},"Validator List"],["p",{},"List of the active validators for the current epoch."],["h2",{"id":"validator-set"},"Validator Set"],["p",{},"List of all the active and potential validators."],["h2",{"id":"zero-knowledge-proof-zkp"},"Zero-Knowledge Proof (ZKP)"],["p",{},"A cryptographic method that allows one party to prove to another that a statement is true without revealing any information beyond the validity of the statement itself. In Nimiq, ZKPs are used for light and full client synchronization."]],"toc":{"title":"","searchDepth":2,"depth":2,"links":[{"id":"account","depth":2,"text":"Account"},{"id":"address","depth":2,"text":"Address"},{"id":"aggregated-signature","depth":2,"text":"Aggregated In our algorithm, we assume that out of 3",["em",{},"f"],"+1 validators, at most ",["em",{},"f"]," are malicious. This assumption ensures valid and accurate performance of the blockchain, even if up to ",["em",{},"f"]," validators fail to respond or act maliciously. An epoch, comprising multiple batches, ends with a closing election macro block. While validators remain constant within an epoch, the election macro block selects new validators for the next epoch."],["h3",{"id":"micro-blocks"},"Micro Blocks"],["p",{},"Produced by selected validators, ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/consensus/block-format#micro-blocks"},"micro blocks"]," contain user transactions. A ",["a",{"href":"validators/skip-blocks"},"skip block"]," may replace a delayed micro block, signed by over two-thirds of validators in the current epoch."],["h3",{"id":"macro-blocks"},"Macro Blocks"],["p",{},"After a set number of micro blocks, a ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/consensus/block-format#macro-blocks"},"macro block"]," finalizes the batch. Randomly proposed by a leader, macro blocks undergo a two-step voting process. Election blocks provide periodic finality, renewing the validator set, while checkpoint blocks retain the validator set."],["h2",{"id":"consensus-mechanism"},"Consensus Mechanism"],["h3",{"id":"tendermint-integration"},"Tendermint Integration"],["p",{},"Albatross uses Tendermint for macro block consensus, ensuring Byzantine fault tolerance. This ensures network continuity and prevents malicious validators from halting the network."],["h2",{"id":"dealing-with-malicious-behavior"},"Dealing with Malicious Behavior"],["p",{},"Validators earn rewards for contributions and face ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/consensus/punishments"},"punishments"]," for consensus violations, with severity varying by offense type. Minor offenses lead to deactivation of the responsible slot and burned rewards. Severe offenses result in a ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/consensus/punishments#jail"},"jail"]," state, where the validator, including all slots, is locked for an extended period, with burned rewards and an inability to be re-elected. The jailing period also affects stakers, as their stake is locked for the duration of the jailing period."],["h3",{"id":"punishment-types"},"Punishment Types"],["table",{},["thead",{},["tr",{},["th",{},"Offense Type"],["th",{},"Consequence"],["th",{},"Duration"],["th",{},"Validator Status"]]],["tbody",{},["tr",{},["td",{},["strong",{},"Block Delay"]],["td",{},"Slot deactivation + reward burning"],["td",{},"Temporary"],["td",{},"Slot suspended"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["strong",{},"Equivocation"]],["td",{},"Jail state + all rewards burned"],["td",{},"8 epochs"],["td",{},"Fully locked"]]]],["h2",{"id":"network-security"},"Network Security"],["u-page-grid",{},["u-page-card",{"description":"Network continues operating even with f faulty validators","icon":"i-nimiq:duotone-network","title":"Liveness","variant":"outline"}],["u-page-card",{"description":"Consensus cannot be reached on conflicting blocks","icon":"i-nimiq:verified","title":"Safety","variant":"outline"}],["u-page-card",{"description":"Once consensus is reached, it cannot be reversed","icon":"i-tabler:lock","title":"Finality","variant":"outline"}]],["h2",{"id":"getting-started"},"Getting Started"],["p",{},"Ready to dive deeper into the Nimiq protocol? │\n└─────────────────────────────────────────┘\n"]],["h2",{"id":"system-components"},"System Components"],["h3",{"id":"coordination-layer"},"Coordination Layer"],["p",{},["strong",{},"Consensus Component"],": Acts as the central orchestrator, analyzing peer agreement and sync progress to determine network consensus state. Emits ",["code",{},"ConsensusEvent::Established"]," and ",["code",{},"ConsensusEvent::Lost"]," to coordinate system behavior."],["p",{},["strong",{},"Syncer Component"],": Manages the macro sync → live sync lifecycle, selecting appropriate strategies based on node configuration and coordinating peer state transitions."],["p",{},["strong",{},"Proxy Architecture"],": Both components use proxy patterns (",["code",{},"ConsensusProxy"],", ",["code",{},"SyncerProxy"],") to provide thread-safe async interfaces while maintaining clear separation between coordination logic and external access."],["p",{},["strong",{},["code",{},"RemoteDataStore"]," and Event Dispatcher"],": Supporting components such as ",["code",{},"RemoteDataStore"]," and ",["code",{},"RemoteEventDispatcher"]," provide remote access to account, validator, and staker data, and enable address-based event subscriptions."],["h3",{"id":"strategy-implementation-layer"},"Strategy Implementation Layer"],["p",{},["strong",{},"Sync Strategies"],": Implement ",["code",{},"MacroSync"]," and ",["code",{},"LiveSync"]," traits to enable different verification approaches and resource optimization patterns. See ",["a",{"href":"traits-and-abstractions"},"Traits and Abstractions"]," for detailed interface specifications."],["p",{},["strong",{},"Trait-Based Design"],": The sync system is built around trait-based abstractions, such as ",["code",{},"MacroSync"],", ",["code",{},"LiveSync"],", and ",["code",{},"LiveSyncQueue"],". This approach allows for pluggable sync strategies, making it straightforward to extend or adapt the system for new node types or future requirements."],["p",{},["strong",{},"Queue Architecture"],": The core of peer request coordination is the generic ",["code",{},"SyncQueue"]," abstraction, which manages requests, retries, and peer rotation for all sync strategies."],["h2",{"id":"communication-architecture"},"Communication Architecture"],["p",{},["strong",{},"Peer Classification Systems"],": Different sync phases use specialized peer classification approaches."],["p",{},["strong",{},"Macro Sync Classification"],":"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Good"],": Successfully synchronized to peer''s macro state"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Outdated"],": Peer is behind local chain or provided outdated information"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Incompatible"],": Peer does not provide required services for synchronization; ",["em",{},"Example: full node cannot serve a syncing history node"]],["li",{},["strong",{},"Conflicting"],": Peer provided conflicting blockchain data (triggers fallback in Pico Sync)"]],["p",{},["strong",{},"Live Sync Classification"],":"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Behind"],": Peer is too far behind our current blockchain position (exceeds tolerance threshold)"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Ahead"],": Peer is too far ahead of our current blockchain position (exceeds window limit)"]],["p",{},["strong",{},"Peer Management"],": Peer management is handled by the ",["code",{},"PeerList"]," abstraction, which tracks active peers, supports efficient peer rotation, and notifies components when peers join or leave the network."],["h3",{"id":"event-driven-coordination"},"Event-Driven Coordination"],["p",{},["strong",{},"Structured Event System"],": All major components communicate through asynchronous event streams, implementing the ",["code",{},"Stream"]," trait. This enables the system to respond promptly to network changes, peer events, and sync progress:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["code",{},"ConsensusEvent"],": System-wide consensus state changes (",["code",{},"Established"],", ",["code",{},"Lost"],")"],["li",{},["code",{},"LiveSyncPushEvent"],": Block processing outcomes (",["code",{},"AcceptedAnnouncedBlock"],", ",["code",{},"AcceptedBufferedBlock"],", ",["code",{},"ReceivedMissingBlocks"],", ",["code",{},"RejectedBlock"],", ",["code",{},"AcceptedChunks"],")"],["li",{},["code",{},"LiveSyncPeerEvent"],": Peer classification (",["code",{},"Behind"],", ",["code",{},"Ahead"],")"],["li",{},["code",{},"NetworkEvent"],": Peer lifecycle management (",["code",{},"PeerJoined"],", ",["code",{},"PeerLeft"],")"],["li",{},["code",{},"BlockchainEvent"],": State transition coordination (",["code",{},"Extended"],", ",["code",{},"Rebranched"],", ",["code",{},"Finalized"],", ",["code",{},"EpochFinalized"],", ",["code",{},"HistoryAdopted"],", ",["code",{},"Stored"],")"],["li",{},["code",{},"RemoteEvent"],": Inter-peer notifications (",["code",{},"InterestingReceipts"],")"]],["p",{},["strong",{},"Cross-Node Communication"],": ",["code",{},"RemoteEventDispatcher"]," enables selective subscriptions between different node types, supporting efficient address-based notifications without full chain monitoring."],["h2",{"id":"performance"},"Performance"],["p",{},["strong",{},"Async Stream Processing"],": All major components implement ",["code",{},"Stream"]," trait for non-blocking, event-driven processing that maintains responsiveness under load."],["p",{},["strong",{},"Concurrency and Parallel Requests"],":\nThe sync system frequently needs to communicate with multiple peers at once, such as when requesting blocks or proofs. To handle this efficiently, it uses Rust’s ",["code",{},"FuturesUnordered"]," collection. This allows the system to manage many asynchronous tasks in parallel and process each result as soon as it becomes available, regardless of the order they were started. By doing so, the node can take advantage of the fastest responses, keep the synchronization pipeline full, and avoid delays caused by slower peers. This approach ensures high throughput and responsiveness during all phases of synchronization."],["p",{},["strong",{},"Parallel Request Management"],": Multi-peer concurrent request coordination with automatic load balancing and failure recovery ensures optimal network utilization."],["p",{},["strong",{},"Resource Management"],": Configurable buffer sizes and connection limits enable tuning for different deployment environments and resource constraints."],["h2",{"id":"security"},"Security"],["p",{},["strong",{},"Cryptographic Verification"],": History and full nodes perform complete cryptographic validation of all received data."],["p",{},["strong",{},"ZKP Security"],": Full and light nodes achieve high security through zero-knowledge proof verification."],["p",{},["strong",{},"Trust-Based Operation"],": Pico nodes use optimistic trust with automatic fallback to cryptographic verification when conflicts are detected."],["h2",{"id":"error-handling"},"Error Handling"],["p",{},["strong",{},"Automatic Peer Rotation"],": Network request failures trigger immediate peer failover with exponential backoff to prevent cascading failures."],["p",{},["strong",{},"Peer Banning"],": Peers that provide invalid data or misbehave are subject to banning or disconnection. This mechanism protects the node from malicious actors and helps maintain the network''s integrity and security."],["p",{},["strong",{},"State Recovery"],": When errors occur pushing blocks or chunks, the system discards pending chunks and requests chunks again starting from the earliest missing key in the local accounts tree state."],["p",{},["strong",{},["code",{},"EitherSyncer"]],": This abstraction enables seamless runtime fallback between sync strategies, such as automatically switching from Pico Macro Sync to Light Macro Sync when conflicts are detected, without disrupting the overall sync process."],["h2",{"id":"consensus-establishment"},"Consensus Establishment"],["p",{},"A node establishes consensus when it achieves both ",["strong",{},"peer connectivity"]," (minimum 3 peers) and ",["strong",{},"state synchronization"]," (complete required data), plus one of:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Network Activity"],": Accepted 5+ block announcements extending the local chain"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Peer Agreement"],": Knowledge of head blocks from 2/3+ of connected peers"]],["p",{},["strong",{},"Head Consensus:"]],["ul",{},["li",{},"Poll multiple peers simultaneously for chain head"],["li",{},"Require 2/3+ agreement on current blockchain state"],["li",{},"Disconnect from peers with significantly different state"]],["p",{},["strong",{},"Multi-Peer Verification and Proof Validation:"]],["ul",{},["li",{},"Request proofs from multiple peers for cross-validation"],["li",{},"Compare responses to detect inconsistencies"],["li",{},"Build consensus from majority agreement"],["li",{},"Ban peers providing invalid data"]]],"toc":{"title":"","searchDepth":2,"depth":2,"links":[{"id":"two-phase-sync-architecture","depth":2,"text":"Two-Phase Sync Processing"],["p",{},"Block Live Sync emits structured events for different scenarios:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["code",{},"AcceptedAnnouncedBlock(hash)"]," - Live block accepted"],["li",{},["code",{},"AcceptedBufferedBlock(hash, buffer_size)"]," - Buffered block applied"],["li",{},["code",{},"ReceivedMissingBlocks(hashes)"]," - Missing blocks successfully on the block height difference and tolerance configuration:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Behind"],": Peer''s announced block is more than ",["code",{},"tolerate_past_max"]," blocks behind our current head"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Ahead"],": Peer''s announced block is more than ",["code",{},"window_ahead_max"]," blocks ahead of our current head"]],["p",{},["strong",{},"System Actions:"]],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Behind peers"],": Excluded from missing block requests but remain connected for potential catch-up"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Ahead peers"],": May trigger missing block requests if the gap is bridgeable, otherwise marked for potential disconnection"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Excessive drift"],": Peers too far ahead or behind may be disconnected to maintain sync efficiency"]],["p",{},"This event-driven approach enables the consensus layer to track synchronization progress and make informed decisions about peer management and sync INSERT INTO _content_docs VALUES ('docs/protocol/node-sync/live-sync/state-live-sync.md', 'State Live Sync', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["p",{},"Every other node type does not use State Live Sync. Instead, they transition to ",["strong",{},["a",{"href":"block-live-sync"},"Block Live Sync"]]," to synchronize both blocks and complete account state simultaneously."],["h2",{"id":"key-characteristics"},"Key Characteristics"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Peer-to-Peer Operation"],": Each peer is handled independently with separate request queues"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Full State Synchronization"],": Maintains complete account state through parallel chunk downloads"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Full Blockchain Exclusive"],": Compatible only with full blockchain instances"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Multi-Layered Architecture"],": Combines ",["code",{},"BlockQueue"],", ",["code",{},"DiffQueue"],", and ",["code",{},"StateQueue"]," for comprehensive sync"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Partial Trie Reconstruction"],": Builds complete state without requiring transaction history"],["li",{},["strong",{},"State Completion Tracking"],": Monitors blockchain state completeness and triggers chunk requests as needed"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Stream-Based"],": Implements the ",["code",{},"Stream"]," trait for asynchronous event processing"]],["h2",{"id":"how-it-works"},"How It Works"],["p",{},"The ",["code",{},"StateLiveSync"]," manages both block and state synchronization through a layered queue system. It processes incoming blocks while simultaneously requesting and applying state chunks in order to eventually reach a complete blockchain state. The sync monitors account tree completeness and automatically triggers state synchronization when gaps are detected."],["p",{},"The ",["code",{},"StateLiveSync"]," follows a dual-request pattern combining block and state synchronization:"],["ol",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"RequestMissingBlocks"]," → Request missing blocks when gaps are detected"],["li",{},["strong",{},"RequestTrieDiff"]," → Request trie diffs for efficient state updates"],["li",{},["strong",{},"RequestChunk"]," → Request state chunks to fill account tree gaps"]],["p",{},"The complete message specifications are documented in the ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/node-sync/network-protocol"},"Network Protocol"]," document."],["h2",{"id":"partial-trie-construction"},"Partial Trie Construction"],["p",{},"The State Live Sync does not download the entire state trie at once. Instead, nodes request and apply small chunks of the trie incrementally. This approach is necessary because blocks continue to be produced while a node is syncing. By the time the full trie was downloaded, it would already be outdated."],["p",{},"Instead, nodes construct a ",["strong",{},"partial trie"]," by:"],["ul",{},["li",{},"Requesting one chunk at a time"],["li",{},"Using the ",["code",{},"end_key"]," from the previous response as the ",["code",{},"start_key"]," for the next request"],["li",{},"Repeating this process until the full trie is reconstructed and matches the current state"]],["p",{},"At the end of the process, the node compares its computed ",["strong",{},"state root"]," against the expected root in the latest block to verify correctness. This approach eliminates the need for transaction history while maintaining complete account state."],["h2",{"id":"architecture-overview"},"Architecture Overview"],["collapsible",{"title":"State Live Sync Struct (click to expand)"},["pre",{"className":"language-rust shiki shiki-themes material-theme-lighter material-theme material-theme-palenight","code":"pub type StateLiveSync<N> = LiveSyncer<N, StateQueue<N>>;\n\npub struct LiveSyncer<N: Network, Q: LiveSyncQueue<N>> {\n    blockchain: BlockchainProxy,\n\n    network: Arc<N>,\n\n    queue: Q,\n\n    /// Vector of pending push operations.\n    pending: VecDeque<BoxFuture<''static, Q::PushResult>>,\n\n    /// Cache for BLS public keys to avoid repetitive uncompressing.\n    bls_cache: Arc<Mutex<BlsCache>>,\n\n    /// Channel used to communicate additional blocks to the queue.\n    /// We use this to wake up the queue and pass in new, unknown blocks\n    /// received in the consensus as part of the head requests.\n    block_tx: mpsc::Sender<BlockAndSource<N>>,\n}\n\npub struct StateQueue<N: Network> {\n    /// Configuration for the block queue.\n    config: QueueConfig,\n\n    /// Reference to the blockchain.\n    blockchain: Arc<RwLock<Blockchain>>,\n\n    /// Reference to the network.\n    network: Arc<N>,\n\n    /// The queue from which we receive blocks and tree diffs.\n    diff_queue: DiffQueue<N>,\n\n    /// The chunk request component.\n    /// We use it to request chunks from up-to-date peers\n    chunk_request_component: ChunkRequestComponent<N>,\n\n    /// Buffered chunks - `block_height -> block_hash -> BlockAndId`.\n    /// There can be multiple blocks at a height if there are forks.\n    /// For each block, we can store multiple chunks.\n    buffer: BTreeMap<u32, HashMap<Blake2bHash, Vec<ChunkAndSource<N>>>>,\n\n    buffer_size: usize,\n\n    /// The block number of the latest macro block. We prune the block buffer when it changes.\n    current_macro_height: u32,\n\n    /// The starting point for the next chunk to be requested. We reset it to `None` when the\n    /// chain of chunks is invalidated, which will force the component to request the next\n    /// chunk starting from the missing range of the blockchain state.\n    /// Invalidation of the chain may happen by an invalid chunk or block, a discarded chunk,\n    /// or a rebranch event.\n    start_key: ChunkRequestState,\n\n    /// The blockchain event stream.\n    blockchain_rx: BoxStream<''static, BlockchainEvent>,\n\n    /// Waiter for the peer list to become nonempty.\n    ///\n    /// Since we only want to dispatch requests from the\n    /// `ChunkRequestComponent` when its peer list is nonempty, we need some\n    /// notification mechanism to wake us up once the list becomes nonempty if\n    /// we find it empty.\n    peers_became_nonempty: Option<BoxFuture<''static, ()>>,\n}\n\n","language":"rust","meta":"","style":""},["code",{"__ignoreMap":""},["span",{"class":"line","line":1},["span",{},"pub type Process"],["h3",{"id":"_1-dual-stream-processing"},"1. Dual Stream Processing"],["p",{},"The node processes two parallel streams:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Block Announcements"],": Via gossipsub block announcements from peers"],["li",{},["strong",{},"State Monitoring"],": Via blockchain events tracking account tree completeness"]],["h3",{"id":"_2-block-reception-buffering"},"2. Block Reception & Buffering"],["p",{},"Incoming blocks are processed through the layered queue system:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"StateQueue"],": Requests corresponding state chunks for each block to maintain trie completeness"],["li",{},["strong",{},"DiffQueue"],": Applies tree diffs for efficient state updates without full reconstruction"],["li",{},["strong",{},"BlockQueue"],": Handles block ordering and gap detection for continuous sync"]],["h3",{"id":"_3-partial-trie-reconstruction"},"3. Partial Trie Reconstruction"],["p",{},"When the account tree is incomplete, the node initiates chunk-based reconstruction:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Gap Discovery"],": Use ",["code",{},"get_missing_accounts_range()"]," to identify missing trie sections"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Sequential Requests"],": Request chunks starting from missing key ranges with up to 5,000 nodes each"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Progressive Building"],": Apply chunks sequentially using ",["code",{},"start_key"]," from previous response as next ",["code",{},"start_key"]],["li",{},["strong",{},"Chunk Buffering"],": Buffer chunks by block height and hash for ordered application"]],["h3",{"id":"_4-block-state-application"},"4. Block & State Application"],["p",{},"Process blocks and chunks in the following order:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Block Validation"],": Standard block signature and intrinsic validation"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Block Push"],": Apply validated blocks to the blockchain"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Chunk Push"],": Apply state chunks via ",["code",{},"commit_chunks()"]," to rebuild account trie"],["li",{},["strong",{},"BLS Cache Updates"],": Update validator key cache for performance optimization"]],["h3",{"id":"_5-state-completion-tracking-diff-mode"},"5. State Completion Tracking & Diff Mode"],["p",{},"Monitor blockchain state completeness and optimize sync strategy:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Completion Detection"],": Mark state sync complete when ",["code",{},"accounts_complete()"]," returns true"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Diff Mode Switch"],": Disable chunk requests and enable diff-only processing for efficiency"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Reset Triggers"],": Restart chunk-based reconstruction on rebranch or state reinitialization"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Buffer Pruning"],": Clean outdated chunks when passing macro blocks to manage memory"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Progress Reporting"],": Emit events for state sync progress and completion"]],["h3",{"id":"_6-rebranch-recovery"},"6. Rebranch Recovery"],["p",{},"Handle blockchain reorganizations gracefully:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"State Validation"],": Check if rebranch caused state incompleteness"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Reset Strategy"],": If incomplete, reset to chunk request mode and re-enable diffs"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Buffer Cleanup"],": Clear invalid chunks and restart trie reconstruction from detected gaps"]],["h2",{"id":"event-processing"},"Event Processing"],["p",{},"State Live Sync emits structured events for different synchronization scenarios:"],["p",{},["strong",{},"Block and State Events:"]],["ul",{},["li",{},["code",{},"AcceptedAnnouncedBlock(hash)"]," - Real-time block accepted"],["li",{},["code",{},"AcceptedBufferedBlock(hash, buffer_size)"]," - Buffered block applied"],["li",{},["code",{},"ReceivedMissingBlocks(hashes)"]," - Missing blocks successfully received"],["li",{},["code",{},"RejectedBlock(hash)"]," - Block validation failed"],["li",{},["code",{},"AcceptedChunks(hash)"]," - State chunks applied for head block"]],["p",{},["strong",{},"Peer Events:"]],["ul",{},["li",{},["code",{},"Behind(peer_id)"]," - Peer is behind our blockchain state"],["li",{},["code",{},"Ahead(peer_id)"]," - Peer is ahead of our blockchain state"]],["p",{},["strong",{},"Peer Classification Logic:"]],["p",{},"Peers are classified based on the block height difference and tolerance configuration:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Behind"],": Peer''s announced block is more than ",["code",{},"tolerate_past_max"]," blocks behind our current head"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Ahead"],": Peer''s announced block is more than ",["code",{},"window_ahead_max"]," blocks ahead of our current head"]],["style",{},"html .light .shiki span {color: var(--shiki-light);background: var(--shiki-light-bg);font-style: var(--shiki-light-font-style);font-weight: var(--shiki-light-font-weight);text-decoration: var(--shiki-light-text-decoration);}html.light .shiki span {color: Head Request"],["p",{},"The node sends a RequestHead message to the peer to obtain its current macro and election block hashes. INSERT INTO _content_docs VALUES ('docs/protocol/storage/history-store.md', 'History Store', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["h3",{"id":"transaction-lifecycle"},"Transaction Lifecycle"],["ol",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Transaction submission:"]," A user creates a transaction and sends it to the network. The transaction is then placed into the validator’s ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/storage/mempool"},"mempool"],". The mempool temporarily holds pending transactions before they are included in a block."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Transaction validation:"]," Validators check the validity store to ensure the transaction has not already been included in a block within the validity window. This prevents duplicate transactions."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Block inclusion:"]," If the transaction is valid, a validator includes it in a new block. Once the block is validated, it is added to the blockchain, officially recording the transaction."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Recording in the History Store:"]," After a block is added to the blockchain, its transactions are moved to the History Store, becoming historic transactions. These transactions are stored in ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/storage/merkle-trees#merkle-mountain-range"},"MMR"]," trees, allowing for efficient inclusion proofs."]],["h3",{"id":"history-store"},"History Store"],["p",{},"The History Store is responsible for keeping track of all transactions that have occurred on the blockchain. It ensures that these transactions are stored to enable efficient proof and retrieval. The History Store includes:"],["ol",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"History trees (MMRs):"]," These trees store the hashes of transactions for each epoch in an MMR structure. They provide a way to prove that a transaction has been included in the blockchain."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Historic transactions:"]," These are detailed records of transactions, indexed by their epoch number and leaf index. They include additional data, such as block numbers and timestamps, that enable efficient retrieval."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Last leaf index table:"]," This table keeps track of the last leaf index for each block number, facilitating efficient updates to the MMR."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Validity store:"]," This component tracks transactions within the validity window and prevents duplicates. Both full and history nodes use it to manage transactions'' validity before they are recorded as historic transactions."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Indexing system:"]," When combined with the indexing system (see below), the History Store enables fast retrieval of specific transactions, allowing nodes to easily query transactions by details such as transaction hash, sender, or recipient."]],["p",{},"A historic transaction is a representation of a transaction that has already been included in a block. It contains additional information such as the block number, timestamp, and data about the transaction, providing a clear record of when and where each transaction occurred. This distinction is important for tracking and verifying the transaction history accurately."],["p",{},"The History Store is connected to each new block through the history tree root, which is updated with every new block. This root can be verified to ensure the integrity of the transaction history."],["h3",{"id":"validity-store-and-validity-window"},"Validity Store and Validity Window"],["p",{},"The validity store is a subcomponent of the History Store used by both full and history nodes to track transactions within the validity window. The validity window is a range of blocks within which a transaction must be included to be considered valid. This mechanism prevents duplicate transactions by ensuring that any given transaction can only be included once within the validity window."],["h3",{"id":"history-store-index"},"History Store Index"],["p",{},"The History Store indexing system was created to enable fast and efficient retrieval of specific transactions from the History Store. While the History Store itself is responsible for recording and maintaining all transactions on the blockchain, the indexing component allows history nodes quick access to this data."],["p",{},"Unlike history nodes, full nodes store only one epoch of blockchain data at a time by default, so they do not require the complex indexing functionality that history nodes use. The indexing system maps transaction hashes to their corresponding locations in the History Store, linking them to specific epoch numbers and leaf indices within the MMR trees. This structure allows for the swift identification and retrieval of transactions, even within large datasets."],["p",{},"History nodes use this indexing system mainly to serve clients seeking their transaction history, providing efficient retrieval and accurate proof of inclusion. Thanks to the transaction history and indexes maintained by these nodes, the system also supports detailed queries, such as retrieving transactions by sender or recipient address."],["h3",{"id":"syncing-and-building-the-history"},"Syncing and Building the History"],["p",{},"When a node syncs with the network using the ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/node-sync/macro-sync/history-macro-sync"},"History Macro Sync"]," protocol, it downloads the history for each epoch, which is essential for reconstructing the transaction history up to the blockchain''s current state. As the node processes and verifies each macro block, it builds its local History Store by storing historic transactions, organizing them into MMR trees, and updating relevant records."],["p",{},"After syncing, the node compares the root of its constructed history tree with the root provided by the current block in the blockchain. If the roots match, it confirms that the node''s History Store is accurate and fully synchronized with the network."]],"toc":{"title":"","searchDepth":2,"depth":2,"links":[{"id":"transaction-lifecycle","depth":3,"text":"Transaction Lifecycle"},{"id":"history-store","depth":3,"text":"History Store"},{"id":"validity-store-and-validity-window","depth":3,"text":"Validity Store and Validity Window"},{"id":"history-store-index","depth":3,"text":"History Store Index"},{"id":"syncing-and-building-the-history","depth":3,"text":"Syncing and Building the History"}]}}', 'The History Store is a component of the blockchain that is responsible for recording and maintaining all transactions that have occurred on the blockchain. This system ensures the integrity and traceability of transactions, serving as both a record-keeping and indexing tool. It allows easy retrieval of transactions, such as locating all transactions associated with a specific address.', 'md', 'i-tabler:database', NULL, '{"matter":{"title":"History Store","description":"The History Store is a component of the blockchain that is responsible for recording and maintaining all transactions that have occurred on the blockchain.
