INSERT INTO _content_docs VALUES ('docs/protocol/storage/mempool.md', 'Mempool', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["h2",{"id":"what-is-a-mempool"},"What is a mempool?"],["p",{},"Transactions are broadcasted among the network, and each validator maintains its own mempool. Upon a validator adding a transaction to the blockchain, other validators must remove that transaction from their respective mempools."],["p",{},"Every block has a predetermined storage capacity for transactions. Validators are motivated to maximize the number of transactions in a block, as they seek to receive transaction fees. However, validators also have the option to produce empty blocks or blocks with fewer transactions than the storage allows. Our protocol imposes no restriction on the quantity of transactions a block can contain, only that it has a ceiling."],["p",{},"Users are encouraged to offer higher fees to accelerate the addition of their transactions to the blockchain. The result of a transaction being added to the mempool is to be then added to the block. The mempool serves the dual purpose of filtering transactions and enabling validators to disregard invalid ones."],["p",{},"The blockchain mempool is divided into two groups that hold different types of transactions:"],["ul",{},["li",{},"Transactions from the ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/validators/staking-contract"},"staking contract"]," are added to a ",["strong",{},"control mempool"]],["li",{},"Transactions from basic, HTLC, and vesting ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/accounts"},"accounts"]," are added to a ",["strong",{},"regular mempool"]]],["p",{},"Control transactions have priority over regular transactions, so they are first added to the block, followed by regular transactions."],["h2",{"id":"how-is-a-transaction-added-to-the-mempool"},"How is a transaction added to the mempool?"],["p",{},"A verification process filters transactions before they are added to the mempool. This verification is made orderly, and validators follow this order:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Signature:"]," This serves as proof of the sender’s identity. If the signature is not valid, the transaction is disregarded."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Validity Window:"]," A specific range of blocks during which a transaction remains valid. After this validity window, the transaction becomes invalid, and validators can disregard it. The starting block height of this window is determined by the specified range."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Known Transaction:"],["ul",{},["li",{},"In the event of a validator being offline for a period, there''s a possibility that upon return, they may attempt to add a transaction to a block that has already been added. To address this, when the validator comes back online, they must download the history sync to receive all transactions from the moment they went offline to the present. Only then can they verify if the transaction they want to include in the block hasn’t already been added."],["li",{},"Another scenario involves a transaction ",["em",{},"x"]," being made, received by all validators, and one validator includes it in a block. The transaction ",["em",{},"x"]," is now considered a known transaction, requiring the other validators to remove it from their individual mempools."]]],["li",{},["strong",{},"Balance:"]," After verifying all necessary steps, the validator checks the user’s balance and all pending transactions for that user in the mempool. The mempool must ensure that the sender has adequate funds to cover at least the transaction fees."]],["p",{},"Note that if the first step returns an invalid signature, the transaction is immediately discarded, and verifying the following steps is unnecessary as the first was already invalid. This means that if, for example, the signature is not valid, the rest of the steps do not need to be verified, and the transaction is immediately discarded."],["p",{},"After the two mempools are fed with transactions respecting the verification process, they are kept on hold and will be added to a block by the elected block producer in the following way:"],["ul",{},["li",{},"Control transactions have priority over regular transactions."],["li",{},"Transactions with higher fees have priority over transactions with lower fees (note that this is not the rule. While high fees encourage validators to add the transaction to the block, nothing in the protocol prevents the validator from adding a transaction with low fees)"],["li",{},"New transactions have priority over older ones."]],["p",{},["img",{"alt":"mempool","src":"/assets/images/protocol/mempool.png"}]],["callout",{"color":"info","icon":"i-tabler-info-circle"},"Note that once the transactions are added to the micro block, they aren’t ordered. The order is made in the mempool."],["p",{},"After a transaction is added, the user''s balance is updated, and the validators must update their mempool accordingly. Note that each validator owns a mempool and broadcasts and verifies transactions constantly. Adding and removing transactions from each mempool is a continuous process. Also, two validators may attempt to add the same transaction to different blocks. In this case, the first transaction to be added is the valid one, and the other validator must discard the respective transaction in their mempool."],["h2",{"id":"how-do-transactions-become-invalid-in-the-mempool"},"How do transactions become invalid in the mempool?"],["p",{},"Even after the verification process has been completed and after the transactions have been added to the mempool, transactions may not succeed in being included in a block, as they can become invalid when in the mempool. As transactions are included in the blockchain, validators first verify if:"],["ol",{},["li",{},"There are ",["strong",{},"expired transactions"],". Similar to the verification step of the validity window, when adding a transaction to the mempool, transactions can expire when on hold. In this case, validators must discard the respective transaction."],["li",{},"There is any ",["strong",{},"transaction already included"]," in a block. A validator might have included a transaction in a block that other validators haven''t detected, or a validator might disconnect from the network, and once reconnecting and downloading the history sync, other validators could already have included that transaction. In this case, as soon as a validator notices the respective transaction, they must discard the one already added."],["li",{},"A ",["strong",{},"fork occurred"]," somewhere in the chain. Suppose a malicious validator forks the chain, and the following elected block producer is also malicious and produces on top of the fork. Eventually, a rational validator will be selected, and the blocks produced maliciously must be reverted. Based on this, as soon as a rational validator notices the fork, the blocks produced maliciously are no longer valid.\nThere are two ways to fix this: (1) transactions are adopted by other validators, and they include them in a block in the longest chain; validators with the respective transaction in their mempool can discard it, and (2) transactions are reverted and must be re-added to the mempool as they were added in the block produced maliciously."],["li",{},"The user''s ",["strong",{},"account balance"]," has changed between the time the transaction was added to the mempool and the time the transaction was about to be added to the block. Similar to verifying the user''s balance before the transaction is added to the mempool, if the user''s balance changes in this period, the transaction becomes invalid as the user''s balance is insufficient. Validators must update the user''s balance in their mempool."]]],"toc":{"title":"","searchDepth":2,"depth":2,"links":[{"id":"what-is-a-mempool","depth":2,"text":"What is a mempool?"},{"id":"how-is-a-transaction-added-to-the-mempool","depth":2,"text":"How is a transaction added to the mempool?"},{"id":"how-do-transactions-become-invalid-in-the-mempool","depth":2,"text":"How do transactions become invalid in the mempool?"}]}}', 'A mempool is a waiting list that keeps transactions on hold until validators add them to the blockchain. Transactions, once filtered, are gathered in the mempool before being selected by validators for inclusion in the next block.', 'md', 'i-tabler:list', NULL, '{"matter":{"title":"Mempool","description":"A mempool is a waiting list that keeps transactions on hold until validators add them to the blockchain. Transactions, once filtered, are gathered in the mempool before being selected by validators for inclusion in the next block."}}', '{"title":"Mempool","order":1}', '/protocol/storage/mempool', '{"title":"Mempool","description":"A mempool is a waiting list that keeps transactions on hold until validators add them to the blockchain. Transactions, once filtered, are gathered in the mempool before being selected by validators for inclusion in the next block."}', 'protocol/storage/mempool', 'kfKW0CVv9PMl4vV3-Jt4YOAbMVsk76XG0WOr1rn-v7c'); -- kfKW0CVv9PMl4vV3-Jt4YOAbMVsk76XG0WOr1rn-v7c
INSERT INTO _content_docs VALUES ('docs/protocol/storage/merkle-trees.md', 'Merkle Trees', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["p",{},"We use two types of trees in our blockchain system:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Merkle Radix Tree"],": stores accounts and balances"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Merkle Mountain Range"],": stores transactions"]],["h2",{"id":"merkle-radix-tree"},"Merkle Radix Tree"],["p",{},"The Merkle Radix Tree is a data structure used in our blockchain system to store and manage accounts and balances efficiently. This tree organizes accounts and their corresponding balances within its leaf nodes, where each leaf node holds the hash of a single account."],["p",{},"Merkle Radix Trees are significantly different from standard Merkle Trees. Leaf nodes with similar prefixes are paired up, while leaf nodes with unique prefixes are called “only child” nodes. Parent nodes in the tree can have multiple children or just one child. When a parent node has only one child, it merges with that child node, optimizing space within the tree. Likewise, as parent nodes can have an only child node, parent nodes can also have up to 16 children. Below is a simplified illustration of a Merkle Radix Tree."],["p",{},["img",{"alt":"Diagram of a Merkle Radix Tree showing merged branches","src":"/assets/images/protocol/merkle.png"}]],["p",{},"When constructing the tree, nodes with similar prefixes are paired to form parent nodes, optimizing space by reducing the number of intermediate nodes. If a node does not have a sibling with a similar prefix, it becomes an \"only child\" node and is merged with its parent node, further optimizing space within the tree."],["p",{},"The process of merging adjacent nodes and creating parent nodes continues iteratively until a single hash value remains at the topmost layer of the tree. This hash value, known as the state root, represents the entire tree''s integrity and serves as proof of the accounts'' validity when stored in every block header."],["h2",{"id":"merkle-mountain-range"},"Merkle Mountain Range"],["p",{},"A Merkle Mountain Range (MMR) is another type of Merkle Tree used in our blockchain, specifically for storing transactions. MMRs are optimized to handle large datasets by organizing transactions into a hierarchical set of trees, collectively forming what is referred to as a \"mountain range.” In Nimiq, the root hash of the MMR, known as the \"history root,\" is stored in every block header and is the cryptographic proof of the transaction history up to that point, providing an immutable record of transactions."],["p",{},"Transactions are hashed and stored as leaf nodes. As transactions are appended to the MMR, their hashes are combined and hashed together with adjacent leaf nodes to form intermediate hashes. These intermediate hashes are then recursively combined and hashed with other intermediate hashes and adjacent leaf nodes until reaching the topmost level of the tree structure."],["p",{},"MMRs are designed to efficiently append new transactions without restructuring the entire tree. When a new transaction occurs, its hash is appended to the MMR as a new leaf node, always added to the tree''s rightmost part. As MMRs include a set of trees within, each tree hash root is called a “peak”. To calculate the final root of the tree, the peaks are hashed from the right, combining their hash values to construct the root hash. These peaks, representing the root hash values of individual trees within the MMR, also serve as reference points for subsets of transaction data. Note that due to the nature of the MMR, it is possible to have a sub-tree consisting of just one node. Below is a simplified illustration of an MMR with 3 sub-trees."],["p",{},["img",{"alt":"Diagram of a Merkle Mountain Range with three peaks","src":"/assets/images/protocol/mmr.png"}]],["p",{},"Unlike Merkle Radix Trees where accounts and balances undergo constant updates, MMRs do not require direct data updates. In case a block is reverted, transactions are deleted from the transaction history, and new transactions are appended as new blocks come in."],["h3",{"id":"peaks-only-merkle-mountain-ranges"},"Peaks-only Merkle Mountain Ranges"],["p",{},"The \"peaks-only MMR\" is a special MMR variant in our blockchain that is exclusive to full nodes. Only the peaks of the MMR are stored, while all other intermediate nodes and leaf nodes are removed. This approach allows full nodes to store only the necessary data to prove the history root, significantly reducing storage requirements. They do not need to store the entire transaction history represented by the MMR, which is computationally heavy."],["p",{},"This special MMR serves as a lightweight and efficient solution for verifying the integrity of transaction data. By storing only the peak nodes, full nodes can significantly reduce the amount of data they need to retain while still being able to prove the correctness of transaction computations. Full nodes can compare the computed history root against the current block history root to verify the blockchain''s integrity."]],"toc":{"title":"","searchDepth":2,"depth":2,"links":[{"id":"merkle-radix-tree","depth":2,"text":"Merkle Radix Tree"},{"id":"merkle-mountain-range","depth":2,"text":"Merkle Mountain Range","children":[{"id":"peaks-only-merkle-mountain-ranges","depth":3,"text":"Peaks-only Merkle Mountain Ranges"}]}]}}', 'In the context of blockchain, ensuring data security is paramount. Our blockchain uses a tree-like structure known as Merkle Trees to store accounts and transactions. This approach guarantees that every piece of data is encoded within these trees, ultimately resulting in a single value called the "root." This root value represents the entire dataset and acts as a commitment to the data being stored in the blocks.', 'md', 'i-tabler:tree', NULL, '{"matter":{"title":"Merkle Trees","description":"In the context of blockchain, ensuring data security is paramount. Our blockchain uses a tree-like structure known as Merkle Trees to store accounts and transactions. This approach guarantees that every piece of data is encoded within these trees, ultimately resulting in a single value called the \"root.\" This root value represents the entire dataset and acts as a commitment to the data being stored in the blocks."}}', '{"title":"Merkle Trees","order":2}', '/protocol/storage/merkle-trees', '{"title":"Merkle Trees","description":"In the context of blockchain, ensuring data security is paramount. Our blockchain uses a tree-like structure known as Merkle Trees to store accounts and transactions. This approach guarantees that every piece of data is encoded within these trees, ultimately resulting in a single value called the \"root.\" This root value represents the entire dataset and acts as a commitment to the data being stored in the blocks."}', 'protocol/storage/merkle-trees', 'SwvfBTZAcAFnwYMtPMY9fm8kDkpXGS8LG3F5iCsoEWc'); -- SwvfBTZAcAFnwYMtPMY9fm8kDkpXGS8LG3F5iCsoEWc
INSERT INTO _content_docs VALUES ('docs/protocol/transactions.md', 'Transactions', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["h2",{"id":"types-of-accounts"},"Types of Accounts"],["p",{},"Nimiq supports four types of accounts, each designed for different purposes:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Basic Account"],": The most commonly used account for standard transactions between individuals or entities"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Hashed Time-Locked Contract (HTLC)"],": Handles conditional transfers, such as atomic swaps, based on cryptographic conditions and time constraints"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Vesting Contract"],": Manages funds released gradually over time, often used to lock funds with a defined release schedule"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Staking Contract"],": Enables validators to lock their NIM, participate in network consensus, and earn rewards"]],["p",{},"Each account type processes transactions differently to meet its specific use case. For more details, refer to the ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/accounts"},"accounts"]," documentation."],["h2",{"id":"transaction-prioritization-and-mempool"},"Transaction Prioritization and Mempool"],["p",{},"Transactions are first placed in the mempool, waiting for validation. Nimiq has two mempools:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Regular Mempool"],": For basic, HTLC, and vesting transactions"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Control Mempool"],": For staking transactions, which are prioritized"]],["p",{},"Once selected by a validator, transactions are validated and included in a block if they meet the protocol’s requirements. For more details on how the mempool operates, refer to the ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/storage/mempool"},"mempool"]," documentation."],["h2",{"id":"transaction-structure"},"Transaction Structure"],["table",{},["thead",{},["tr",{},["th",{},["strong",{},"Field"]],["th",{},["strong",{},"Data Type"]],["th",{},["strong",{},"Description"]]]],["tbody",{},["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"sender"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Address"]],["td",{},"The sender’s address"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"sender_type"]],["td",{},["code",{},"AccountType"]],["td",{},"Specifies the ",["a",{"href":"#types-of-accounts"},"type"]," of the sender account"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"sender_data"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Vec<u8>"]],["td",{},"Additional data specific to the sender account’s type, primarily used for extra info in transactions from the ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/validators/staking-contract"},"staking contract"]]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"recipient"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Address"]],["td",{},"The recipient’s address"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"recipient_type"]],["td",{},["code",{},"AccountType"]],["td",{},"Specifies the type of the recipient account"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"recipient_data"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Vec<u8>"]],["td",{},"Additional data specific to the recipient account. Used in transactions involving contracts such as staking, HTLCs, and vesting contracts."]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"value"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Coin"]],["td",{},"The amount of NIM to transfer. This field can be zero for transactions that do not transfer value but interact with the staking contract"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"fee"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Coin"]],["td",{},"The fee paid by the sender for the transaction. Validators earn this fee as part of the block rewards for including the transaction in the blockchain as an incentive"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"validity_start_height"]],["td",{},["code",{},"u32"]],["td",{},"The block height from which the transaction becomes valid. A transaction can be submitted before this height, but will only be eligible for inclusion once the blockchain reaches this height. If not included in a block within the window of 120 blocks after, the transaction will expire"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"network_id"]],["td",{},["code",{},"NetworkId"]],["td",{},"Specifies the network (Mainnet, Testnet) on which the transaction is valid to ensure the transaction is only processed on the correct network"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"flags"]],["td",{},["code",{},"TransactionFlags"]],["td",{},"Flags that indicate special transaction types, such as contract creation or signaling"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["code",{},"proof"]],["td",{},["code",{},"Vec<u8>"]],["td",{},"A cryptographic proof, generated by signing the transaction data with the sender’s private key, which authenticates the sender"]]]],["p",{},["strong",{},"Additional Considerations"]],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Variations in Transaction Structure"],": Different account types (basic, HTLC, vesting, staking) may have variations in their transaction structure. These variations ensure that each account type processes transactions according to its specific purpose and function within the protocol"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Incoming and Outgoing Transactions"],": The network distinguishes between incoming and outgoing transactions. ",["strong",{},"Incoming transactions"]," are related to operations within an account, such as adding stake or creating an account. ",["strong",{},"Outgoing transactions"]," involve external account operations that affect the network state, such as deleting a validator or removing stake"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Zero-Value Transactions"],": Staking transactions can have a zero ",["code",{},"value"]," field for contract interaction without fund transfer"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Serialization and Deserialization"],": Transactions are ",["strong",{},"serialized"]," to reduce size for transmission and ",["strong",{},"deserialized"]," by nodes for validation and processing across the network"]],["p",{},"For a deeper dive into the transactions and serialization specifics, refer to the document ",["a",{"href":"https://gist.github.com/sisou/33ece69190cf38f884b1781ad9d5a106","rel":["nofollow"]},"Albatross Transaction Serialization"],"."],["h2",{"id":"transaction-flow-and-potential-issues"},"Transaction Flow and Potential Issues"],["p",{},"Transactions go through multiple stages of verification before being added to a block, and there are potential issues that might arise at each stage:"],["ol",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Transaction Builder Errors"],": These occur when creating a transaction and required fields (such as the sender, recipient, or value) are missing or invalid"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Transaction Errors"],": These errors happen when the transaction is submitted to the network, where the transaction fails checks like invalid proof, wrong network, or serialization errors"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Failed Transactions"],": Even after the previous steps have been successfully verified with no errors, a transaction can fail if the sender lacks sufficient funds to cover the transaction value or the fees"]],["h3",{"id":"transaction-builder-errors"},"Transaction Builder Errors"],["p",{},"Deals with missing or invalid fields when creating transactions. If any of the following errors occur, the transaction will not even be sent:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"No Sender"],": The sender address is missing from the transaction"],["li",{},["strong",{},"No Recipient"],": The recipient address is missing. A valid recipient must be provided"],["li",{},["strong",{},"No Value"],": The transaction value (amount to be transferred) is missing"],["li",{},["strong",{},"No Validity Start Height"],": The transaction’s validity start height, which defines from when the transaction becomes valid, is not set"],["li",{},["strong",{},"No Network ID"],": The transaction’s network ID is missing. The ID identifies which network the transaction is intended, whether Testnet or Mainnet"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Invalid Sender"],": The sender is not valid for the recipient"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Invalid Value"],": For signaling transactions, the value must be zero, whereas other transactions require a non-zero value. This error is triggered when the value does not match the requirements for the specific transaction type"]],["h3",{"id":"transaction-error"},"Transaction Error"],["p",{},"Deals with transaction execution issues when it is submitted to the network:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Foreign Network"],": The transaction is intended for a network other than the one it was submitted to (a transaction for the Testnet being submitted to the Mainnet)"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Zero Value"],": The transaction’s value is set to zero when it’s not allowed. Transactions that transfer funds must have a non-zero value unless they are specific contract interactions that permit zero-value transactions"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Invalid Value"],": The value of the transaction is not valid for the specific operation being attempted (incorrect value for contract-related transactions)"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Overflow"],": The transaction causes an overflow in calculations, due to large values that cannot be processed"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Sender Equals Recipient"],": The sender and recipient of the transaction are the same which is not allowed"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Invalid For Sender"],": The transaction is not valid for the sender’s account type or current state"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Invalid Proof"],": The cryptographic proof for the transaction is incorrect, meaning the transaction was not correctly signed by the sender"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Invalid For Recipient"],": The transaction is not valid for the recipient’s account type or current state"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Invalid Data"],": The transaction contains invalid data, which could refer to incorrect parameters or malformed data fields"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Invalid Serialization"],": There is an issue with the serialization of the transaction, typically occurring during the encoding or decoding process"]],["h3",{"id":"failed-transactions"},"Failed Transactions"],["p",{},"The final verification of a transaction occurs before it enters the mempool. Even if it passes earlier stages, it can fail if the sender lacks sufficient funds. Validators ensure the sender can cover the transaction fees before adding it to the mempool. If the sender does not even have enough for the fees, the transaction is deemed invalid and not processed."],["p",{},"Including a transaction in a block may succeed or fail:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Successful"],": The sender has enough funds to cover both the transaction value and fees, and the user''s balance is updated"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Failed"],": The sender has insufficient funds to pay out the transaction value but transaction fees can be deducted"]],["p",{},["strong",{},"Example Scenario"]],["p",{},"Consider a scenario where Alice has 100 NIM in her balance. She sends 3 transactions:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Transaction 1"],": Alice sends 80 NIM to Bob. Since she has sufficient funds (80 NIM + fees), this transaction succeeds, and both the value and fees are deducted from her account"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Transaction 2"],": Alice attempts to send 50 NIM to Charlie immediately after. Since her remaining balance is insufficient to cover both the value and fees, this transaction fails, but the transaction fees are still deducted"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Transaction 3"],": Alice tries to send another transaction but does not have enough NIM to cover the fee. In this case, the transaction is deemed ",["strong",{},"invalid"]," and is not processed by the network. Neither the value nor the fees are deducted"]],["h2",{"id":"transaction-finality"},"Transaction Finality"],["p",{},"Finality is periodically reinforced through the Tendermint protocol, which ensures that blocks and the transactions within them cannot be reversed once they are finalized by a macro block. For more details on how finality is achieved through the block structure, please refer to the ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/consensus/block-format"},"block format"]," documentation."],["h2",{"id":"inherents"},"Inherents"],["p",{},"In addition to user-initiated transactions, Nimiq also processes ",["strong",{},"inherents"],"—system-generated operations that modify the blockchain’s state without requiring a transaction from a user. These are used for tasks like distributing rewards and enforcing punishments, ensuring network stability."],["p",{},["strong",{},"Key Characteristics of Inherents:"]],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"No Account Interaction"],": Inherents don’t originate from user accounts or affect balances"],["li",{},["strong",{},"System-Driven"],": These operations are generated by the protocol itself based on network rules and conditions"],["li",{},["strong",{},"No Signature Requirement"],": Inherents don’t require digital signatures for validation."]],["p",{},"There are 5 types of inherents in the Nimiq protocol:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Reward"],": Automatically issued to validators who successfully fulfill their assigned slots without misbehavior"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Penalty"],": Applied to validators who delay block production. The penalty removes their reward for the slot, and the validator can be deactivated"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Jail"],": Enforced when validators are involved in severe misbehavior, such as double voting or creating forks. In this case, all of their slots are punished, and the validator is jailed, preventing further participation in the network for 8 epochs"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Finalize Batch"],": Triggered at the end of each batch of micro blocks, marking its completion"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Finalize Epoch"],": Occurs at the end of an epoch, marking a significant period in the blockchain''s state updates. This ensures that all transactions and state changes for the epoch are finalized and the validator list is updated"]]],"toc":{"title":"","searchDepth":2,"depth":2,"links":[{"id":"types-of-accounts","depth":2,"text":"Types of Accounts"},{"id":"transaction-prioritization-and-mempool","depth":2,"text":"Transaction Prioritization and Mempool"},{"id":"transaction-structure","depth":2,"text":"Transaction Structure"},{"id":"transaction-flow-and-potential-issues","depth":2,"text":"Transaction Flow and Potential Issues","children":[{"id":"transaction-builder-errors","depth":3,"text":"Transaction Builder Errors"},{"id":"transaction-error","depth":3,"text":"Transaction Error"},{"id":"failed-transactions","depth":3,"text":"Failed Transactions"}]},{"id":"transaction-finality","depth":2,"text":"Transaction Finality"},{"id":"inherents","depth":2,"text":"Inherents"}]}}', 'Transactions modify the state of the Nimiq blockchain according to protocol rules and enable value transfers and contract interactions. INSERT INTO _content_docs VALUES ('docs/protocol/validators/skip-blocks.md', 'Skip Blocks', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["p",{},"The primary purpose of skip blocks is to ensure blockchain continuity by serving a structural role rather than a transactional one. Unlike micro blocks, which process transactions, skip blocks are like placeholders that prevent interruptions when a validator fails to produce a block."],["p",{},"Skip blocks are canonical, meaning their structure is strictly determined by the protocol. For any given block, the skip block that follows is determined by the protocol rules. Once a skip block is added, the chain resumes regular micro block production with one validator per block. There is no maximum limit on the number of skip blocks, as skip blocks adapt to the chain’s continuity."],["h3",{"id":"skip-block-and-micro-block-differences"},"Skip Block and Micro Block Differences"],["p",{},"Skip blocks are a special type of micro block, but because they do not hold transactions, there are some key differences. Below are the main differences between a skip block and a regular micro block:"],["table",{},["thead",{},["tr",{},["th",{},["strong",{},"Feature"]],["th",{},["strong",{},"Skip Block"]],["th",{},["strong",{},"Micro Block"]]]],["tbody",{},["tr",{},["td",{},["strong",{},"Body"]],["td",{},"Empty - skip blocks have no transactions or equivocation proof data"],["td",{},"Contains transaction data and possibly equivocation proofs"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["strong",{},"Body Root"]],["td",{},"The ",["code",{},"body_root"]," field in the block header is the hash of the empty body"],["td",{},"The ",["code",{},"body_root"]," is the hash of the body, which includes the transactions and equivocation proofs"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["strong",{},"Timestamp"]],["td",{},"Derived from the previous block’s timestamp plus a fixed duration (4 seconds)"],["td",{},"Generated based on the block’s production time by the assigned producer"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["strong",{},"Extra Data"]],["td",{},"Must be empty to ensure the skip block is canonical"],["td",{},"Can include additional data as required by the transactions or block producer"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["strong",{},"VRF Seed"]],["td",{},"Inherits the VRF from the previous micro block without introducing new randomness"],["td",{},"A new VRF seed is generated by the block producer introducing new randomness"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["strong",{},"Justification"]],["td",{},"Aggregated signatures from multiple validators"],["td",{},"Single signature from the assigned producer"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["strong",{},"Producer"]],["td",{},"No specific producer; multiple validators attest to the skip block"],["td",{},"Block producer selected to produce the block"]]]],["img",{"className":["object-contain","max-h-[max(80vh,220px)]"],"src":"/assets/images/protocol/skip-micro.png","alt":"skip block comparison to micro blocks"}],["h3",{"id":"how-is-the-skip-block-added"},"How is the Skip Block Added?"],["p",{},"A skip block is an agreement among validators to confirm they did not see a micro block in the expected timeframe. The block’s justification consists of the aggregated signatures from the validators. The process goes as follows:"],["ol",{},["li",{},"The elected validator fails to produce a micro block within the expected timeframe"],["li",{},"Upon noticing the missing block, any validator can create a skip block locally"],["li",{},"Validators each create and sign their skip block, then share their signatures with peers"],["li",{},"When at least 2",["em",{},"f"],"+1 signatures are collected, they are aggregated into the skip block proof"],["li",{},"The skip block is added and the chain resumes regular block production with the next validator. If the next validator fails, the process above repeats"]],["p",{},["strong",{},"There are only two outcomes for a delayed micro block:"]],["ul",{},["li",{},"Receive 2",["em",{},"f"],"+1 signatures to a skip block, add it to the chain, and resume block production"],["li",{},"Wait to receive a micro block; if a validator does not receive a skip block, it means that at least 2",["em",{},"f"],"+1 validators saw the expected micro block, and the remainder should receive it shortly"]],["h3",{"id":"validator-penalty-for-misbehavior"},"Validator Penalty for Misbehavior"],["p",{},"A delay in block production is considered a minor offense. When a validator fails to produce a micro block in time, the associated slot is marked to not receive rewards, which are burned as a penalty. This delay also results in the deactivation of the validator slot, although it can reactivate itself after one block. For more information on this misbehavior, see the ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/consensus/punishments#block-production-delay"},"punishments"]," document."]],"toc":{"title":"","searchDepth":2,"depth":2,"links":[{"id":"skip-block-and-micro-block-differences","depth":3,"text":"Skip Block and Micro Block Differences"},{"id":"how-is-the-skip-block-added","depth":3,"text":"How is the Skip Block Added?"},{"id":"validator-penalty-for-misbehavior","depth":3,"text":"Validator Penalty for Misbehavior"}]}}', 'Skip blocks are a special type of micro block in the consensus protocol. When a validator fails to produce a block within the expected timeframe, the remaining elected validators can collectively agree to add a skip block instead of the missing micro block. This prevents delays and ensures the blockchain progresses smoothly. Once a supermajority of elected validators agrees to add the skip block, the chain resumes its regular progression.', 'md', 'i-tabler:skip-forward', NULL, '{"matter":{"title":"Skip Blocks","description":"Skip blocks are a special type of micro block in the consensus protocol. When a validator fails to produce a block within the expected timeframe, the remaining elected validators can collectively agree to add a skip block instead of the missing micro block. This prevents delays and ensures the blockchain progresses smoothly. Once a supermajority of elected validators agrees to add the skip block, the chain resumes its regular progression."}}', '{"title":"Skip Blocks","order":6}', '/protocol/validators/skip-blocks', '{"title":"Skip Blocks","description":"Skip blocks are a special type of micro block in the consensus protocol. When a validator fails to produce a block within the expected timeframe, the remaining elected validators can collectively agree to add a skip block instead of the missing micro block. This prevents delays and ensures the blockchain progresses smoothly. INSERT INTO _content_docs VALUES ('docs/protocol/validators/slots.md', 'Slots', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["p",{},"Upon selection, validators are assigned a certain number of slots, which serve as the means to produce blocks. The allocation of slots to a validator depends on the NIM they have deposited in the staking contract. Validators participate in the consensus process by producing micro blocks, proposing and voting for macro blocks, attesting to malicious behavior from other validators, and thus ensuring an agreement on the current blockchain state."],["p",{},"Validators use their slots in the following way:"],["ul",{},["li",{},"One slot is used to produce one micro block"],["li",{},"One slot is used to propose a macro block"],["li",{},"All validators’ slots are used to vote for a macro block proposal or a ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/validators/skip-blocks"},"skip block"]]],["table",{},["thead",{},["tr",{},["th",{},"Validator address"],["th",{},"Validator pubkey"],["th",{},"Range of slots"]]],["tbody",{},["tr",{},["td",{},"Validator 1"],["td",{},"pubkey 1"],["td",{},"(0, 24)"]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Validator 2"],["td",{},"pubkey 2"],["td",{},"(25, 84)"]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Validator 3"],["td",{},"pubkey 3"],["td",{},"(85, 149)"]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Validator 4"],["td",{},"pubkey 4"],["td",{},"(150, 254)"]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Validator 5"],["td",{},"pubkey 5"],["td",{},"(255, 304)"]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Validator 6"],["td",{},"pubkey 6"],["td",{},"(305, 321)"]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Validator 7"],["td",{},"pubkey 7"],["td",{},"(322, 361)"]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Validator 8"],["td",{},"pubkey 8"],["td",{},"(362, 436)"]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Validator 9"],["td",{},"pubkey 9"],["td",{},"(437, 481)"]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Validator 10"],["td",{},"pubkey 10"],["td",{},"(482, 512)"]]]],["p",{},"Due to the redistribution of the slot owner list with every micro block, it is impossible to anticipate which validator will be responsible for producing the next block. The same validator may end up producing three consecutive blocks if the range is extensive. For instance, based on the above figure, if slots 151, 170, and 237 were chosen to produce the following three blocks, the resulting range would correspond to validator 4."],["p",{},"Note that whether a validator signs a block with slot x or y, it uses its validator key. Validators do not have keys per slot but keys per validator."],["h2",{"id":"validators-list-selection"},"Validators List Selection"],["p",{},"The list of validators for the next epoch is initially chosen in the election block that closes the previous epoch. The block leader generates this list using entropy from the VRF seed of the previous block. The new list contains the validators for the new epoch, along with the distribution of slots for each validator, given a range."],["h2",{"id":"slot-owner-selection"},"Slot Owner Selection"],["p",{},"Subsequently, to select the producer of the first block of the epoch, the entropy of the VRF seed of the previous block is used to shuffle the validator list. This slot owner list is randomly generated for every micro block, determining the producer of the upcoming micro block."],["h2",{"id":"random-seed-generation"},"Random Seed Generation"],["p",{},"The VRF seed serves two purposes for validator selection: providing the randomness needed to select validators based on their stake and determining the slot owners for block production. The initial random seed is generated from an external source at the genesis block. Subsequently, for generating random seeds for subsequent blocks, the protocol relies on the ",["a",{"href":"https://www.signal.org/docs/specifications/xeddsa/#vxeddsa","rel":["nofollow"]},"VXEDdSA"]," algorithm implemented as a verifiable random function. In addition to producing and proposing blocks, validators are also responsible for generating the random seed included in every block. The random seed from the election macro block is used to generate the validator list for the epoch, while the random seed from the previous block is used to reallocate slots and elect the slot owner for each micro block."]],"toc":{"title":"","searchDepth":2,"depth":2,"links":[{"id":"validators-list-selection","depth":2,"text":"Validators List Selection"},{"id":"slot-owner-selection","depth":2,"text":"Slot Owner Selection"},{"id":"random-seed-generation","depth":2,"text":"Random Seed Generation"}]}}', 'Validators are selected from the validator set to integrate the following validator list based on their initial stake. Validators that have deposited a higher stake increase their chances of being selected for the upcoming epoch. At the end of an epoch, the block leader proposes a new validator list in the election macro block, including the group of validators eligible to participate in the consensus.', 'md', 'i-tabler:calendar', NULL, '{"matter":{"title":"Slots","description":"Validators are selected from the validator set to integrate the following validator list based on their initial stake. INSERT INTO _content_docs VALUES ('docs/protocol/validators/stakers.md', 'Stakers', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["div",{"className":["columns"]},["div",{},["h3",{"id":"stake-states"},"Stake States"],["p",{},["strong",{},"Active"],": Funds actively participating in staking"],["p",{},["strong",{},"Inactive"],": Funds temporarily inactive, not actively participating, awaiting either reactivation or retirement"],["p",{},["strong",{},"Retired"],": Funds permanently marked for withdrawal, not participating in staking anymore"],["p",{},["strong",{},"Removed"],": Funds completely withdrawn from the staking contract"]],["div",{},["h3",{"id":"balances"},"Balances"],["p",{},["strong",{},"Active balance"],": The portion of the staker''s funds currently used by the validator as stake"],["p",{},["strong",{},"Inactive balance"],": The portion of the staker''s funds that is not currently being used as stake and is locked for a period or inactive"],["p",{},["strong",{},"Retired balance"],": The portion of the staker''s funds that exists but is ready to be removed from the staking contract"],["p",{},["strong",{},"Non-retired balance"],": The sum of the active and inactive balances"],["p",{},["strong",{},"Total balance"],": The sum of the active, inactive, and retired balances"]]],["h3",{"id":"state-transitions"},"State Transitions"],["table",{},["thead",{},["tr",{},["th",{},"From State"],["th",{},"To State"],["th",{},"Conditions/Notes"]]],["tbody",{},["tr",{},["td",{},"Active"],["td",{},"Inactive"],["td",{},"During this period, funds are locked and cannot be moved or withdrawn. The lock-up period accounts for any potential misbehavior by a validator. The released block is the block at which this period ends, and the staker funds are fully released if no jailing occurs"]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Inactive"],["td",{},"Active"],["td",{},"Immediate transition"]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Inactive"],["td",{},"Retired"],["td",{},"Can retire a specific amount if leaving a balance greater than the minimum stake in active + inactive. To retire all, move all active + inactive to retired"]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Retired"],["td",{},"Removed"],["td",{},"Remove all funds in the retired balance; partial removal is not allowed. Funds can only be removed after they have been retired"]]]],["p",{},"Stakers must wait through the reporting window or the remaining jail period of the validator, whichever is longer, to inactivate their funds. The inactive balance can only be released after the lock-up period or the validator''s jail period has passed. If the validator is not jailed, stakers must wait for one epoch of reporting window counting from the next election block."],["p",{},["strong",{},"Examples"]],["ul",{},["li",{},"Suppose a validator has a remaining jail period of 3 epochs and the reporting window is 2 epochs. In that case, the staker must wait for 3 epochs (the longer period) before the funds are released."],["li",{},"If 1 epoch remains of the jailing period while the reporting window can last up to 2 epochs, the staker must wait for the full reporting window period before the funds are released, as the longest release block prevails."]],["h3",{"id":"transactions"},"Transactions"],["p",{},["strong",{},"Create Staker"]],["p",{},"Creates a new staker with a specified address and optional delegation. If no validator is added, the staker is not actively delegating its stake to a validator and thus is not eligible to receive rewards. The initial stake (minimum 100 NIM) is placed in the active balance."],["p",{},["strong",{},"Add Stake"]],["p",{},"Adds coins to the staker’s active balance from any external address. The resulting non-retired funds must meet the minimum stake requirements."],["p",{},["strong",{},"Set Active Stake"]],["p",{},"Sets the desired active stake, which automatically adjusts the inactive balance accordingly. For example, if a staker has 500 NIM and sets the active stake to 300 NIM, the inactive stake will be adjusted to 200 NIM. The inactive balance then becomes locked to account for potential validator misbehavior."],["p",{},["strong",{},"Update Staker"]],["p",{},"Updates the validator address to which the stake is delegated. This process involves several steps:"],["ol",{},["li",{},"The staker must set the active stake to 0, making all non-retired funds inactive"],["li",{},"The staker must wait for the inactive balance to be released, which involves waiting for one epoch + the blocks left in the current epoch"],["li",{},"Once the inactive funds are released, the staker can update the validator address"],["li",{},"Finally, the staker needs to return the inactive stake to active as desired"]],["p",{},"Alternatively, stakers have an option in their configuration to activate a setting that automatically reactivates their stake after the reporting window time when executing this transaction."],["p",{},["strong",{},"Retire Stake"]],["p",{},"Moves funds from inactive to retired balance, making them eligible for withdrawal. Only inactive funds released (post lock-up period) can be retired. A staker can either retire all the non-retired stake or leave at least the minimum stake in the non-retired balance; otherwise, the transaction fails."],["p",{},["strong",{},"Remove Stake"]],["p",{},"Withdraws the retired balance from a staker''s account, removing it from the staking contract. The transaction must remove the entire retired balance; partial removals are not allowed. If the total balance drops to zero, the staker''s account is deleted."],["p",{},"Transactions to activate, inactivate, and update stakes only take effect at the next election block. This is because validator balances, which include staker balances, cannot be changed during an epoch. For update and set active stake transactions, the reporting window time begins when the transaction is sent, but the changes to the funds are only reflected in the validator''s balance at the next election block."],["img",{"className":["object-contain","max-h-[max(80vh,220px)]"],"src":"/assets/images/protocol/stakers-state.png","alt":"stakers states"}],["h3",{"id":"invariants"},"Invariants"],["p",{},"There is a set of invariants or rules that ensure the transactions mentioned above do not fail. The following table outlines these key invariants and the transactions they affect:"],["table",{},["thead",{},["tr",{},["th",{},"Invariant"],["th",{},"Description"],["th",{},"Affected Transactions"]]],["tbody",{},["tr",{},["td",{},"Minimum stake for non-retired balances"],["td",{},"Active + inactive balances must be equal to or greater than the minimum stake if the non-retired balance is not zero"],["td",{},"Create Staker, Add Stake, Set Active Stake, Retire Stake"]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Minimum stake for total balances"],["td",{},"Total balance (active + inactive + retired) must be equal to or greater than the minimum stake if the total balance is not zero"],["td",{},"Create Staker, Add Stake, Retire Stake"]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Inactive balance and block height association"],["td",{},"An associated block height is required for any inactive balance"],["td",{},"Set Active Stake, Update Staker, Retire Stake"]],["tr",{},["td",{},"Validator lock periods"],["td",{},"Inactive funds are subject to lock periods based on validator status. If the validator is jailed, the longest period between the reporting window and the jail period applies"],["td",{},"Set Active Stake, Update Staker, Retire Stake"]]]],["h3",{"id":"edge-cases"},"Edge Cases"],["p",{},["strong",{},"Resetting the reporting window"],": When a staker updates their inactive stake, the reporting window counter resets. For example, if there were 5 blocks left, it would reset at the next election block plus one epoch. This ensures that any potential misconduct by the validator during the initial period is still accounted for, maintaining the integrity of the staking process."],["p",{},["strong",{},"Immediate inactivation and retirement without a validator"],": If a staker does not have a validator associated with them and wants to inactivate and retire their stake, they can do so immediately. Without an associated validator, there is no lock on their funds."],["p",{},["strong",{},"Removing stake requirements"],": A staker can only remove their stake by completely withdrawing all the funds in the retired balance. The staker must first move all desired stake from active to inactive, then retire it before they can remove it. Partial removal from the retired balance is not allowed. However, a staker can retire a specific amount and leave a balance greater than the minimum stake in the non-retired states."],["p",{},["strong",{},"Waiting period with validator in tombstone"],": If a validator is in a tombstone, the staker still needs to wait the reporting window to inactivate their stake."],["p",{},["strong",{},"Updating staker with jailed validator"],": If a staker updates their delegation (with inactive funds) to a new validator that is jailed, they can still convert their funds to active. However, the system treats these funds as if they were attached to the validator when the jailing occurred, so the staker must wait for the release (the longer between the reporting window or the jailed period). The staker’s funds will be considered jailed even if they were inactive when the validator misbehaved."],["p",{},["strong",{},"Adding stake with zero balance"],": If the staker''s active and inactive balances are zero and the staker sends an add stake transaction, they must add more than the minimum stake; otherwise, the invariant is violated."]],"toc":{"title":"","searchDepth":2,"depth":2,"links":[{"id":"state-transitions","depth":3,"text":"State Transitions"},{"id":"transactions","depth":3,"text":"Transactions"},{"id":"invariants","depth":3,"text":"Invariants"},{"id":"edge-cases","depth":3,"text":"Edge Cases"}]}}', 'A staker delegates its NIM to a validator, which validates blocks on its behalf. INSERT INTO _content_docs VALUES ('docs/protocol/validators/staking-contract.md', 'Staking Contract', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["p",{},"The ",["strong",{},"staking contract"]," includes three fields:"],["code-group",{},["pre",{"className":"language-rust shiki shiki-themes material-theme-lighter material-theme material-theme-palenight","code":"pub struct StakingContract {\n    pub balance: Coin,\n    pub active_validators: BTreeMap<Address, Coin>,\n    pub punished_slots: PunishedSlots,\n}\n","language":"rust","meta":"","style":""},["code",{"__ignoreMap":""},["span",{"class":"line","line":1},["span",{},"pub struct StakingContract {\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":2},["span",{},"    pub balance: Coin,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":3},["span",{},"    pub active_validators: BTreeMap<Address, Coin>,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":4},["span",{},"    pub punished_slots: PunishedSlots,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":5},["span",{},"}\n"]]]]],["h2",{"id":"balance"},"Balance"],["p",{},"The ",["code",{},"balance"]," field represents the total amount of coins staked within the ",["code",{},"StakingContract"],". This includes not only the validators'' deposits but also the coins delegated by the stakers."],["h2",{"id":"active-validators"},"Active Validators"],["p",{},"Validators marked as active. All the validator nodes in the network that ",["em",{},"can"]," be elected as block producers for upcoming epochs, as well as the ones that are actively participating in the block production."],["p",{},"This set is stored in a binary tree map that efficiently organizes active validators and their corresponding balances. This structure ensures that only eligible validators, those qualified to receive slots, are included. In cases of misbehavior, validators can either be deactivated or jailed based on the severity of their offense, leading to their removal from this set. Nonetheless, a validator can be excluded from this set and continue to participate in block production until the epoch concludes, as there is no mid-epoch voting process to substitute the validator slots necessary for the consensus."],["h2",{"id":"punished-slots"},"Punished Slots"],["p",{},"The slots marked as punished. Depending on the nature of their misbehavior, validators may have either one slot or all slots marked as punished. The ",["code",{},"punished_slots"]," set keeps track of these punished slots for both the current and previous batch."],["p",{},"In the reward distribution phase, the staking contract cross-verifies slots from the previous batch''s ",["code",{},"punished_slots"]," to identify and burn rewards linked to those specific slots. Additionally, at every macro block, the staking contract determines the block producers for the next batch. Slots marked as punished are excluded from consideration in the selection of block producers for subsequent batches."],["h2",{"id":"staking-contract-account"},"Staking Contract Account"],["p",{},"The staking contract is part of the AccountsTrie and serves as a subtrie containing different account types, each responsible for storing specific staking-related data. The distinct path to each account simplifies navigation within the staking contract subtrie, enabling easy access to various pieces of information. INSERT INTO _content_docs VALUES ('docs/protocol/validators/validator-keys.md', 'Validator Keys', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["p",{},"A key pair consists of public and private keys, mathematically linked. The private key is used for authentication by the validator, while the corresponding public key is used to validate the authenticity of the validator. Validators use their private key to sign transactions. Then anyone can verify the validity of such a transaction by using the validator''s corresponding public key."],["p",{},"For a node to become a validator, it must generate an address and a set of keypairs. Validators also own a fee key from a basic account, which has the sole purpose of paying ",["code",{},"automatic_reactivate"]," transaction fees. To learn how to generate these keys, click ",["a",{"href":"../../nodes//validators//becoming-a-validator#generating-your-validator-address-and-keys"},"here"],"."],["p",{},"The ",["a",{"href":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Schnorr_signature","rel":["nofollow"]},"Schnorr"]," signature scheme is used for generating the cold and warm key pairs, providing simplicity and short signatures. The ",["a",{"href":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BLS_digital_signature","rel":["nofollow"]},"BLS"]," signature scheme is used for the hot key for signature aggregation and voting efficiency."],["h2",{"id":"cold-key"},"Cold Key"],["ul",{},["li",{},"The validator’s address is derived from the cold public key"],["li",{},"The cold private key is used for the validator to sign create, update and delete transactions"]],["h2",{"id":"warm-key"},"Warm Key"],["ul",{},["li",{},"The warm public key is stored in the validator as the ",["code",{},"signing_key"]],["li",{},"The warm private key is used for the validator to sign reactivate, and deactivate transactions"],["li",{},"The warm private key is also used for the validator to sign micro blocks, macro block proposals and generate random seeds"]],["h2",{"id":"hot-key"},"Hot Key"],["ul",{},["li",{},"The hot public key is stored in the validator as the ",["code",{},"voting_key"]],["li",{},"The hot private key is used for the validator to vote for macro block proposals and skip blocks"]],["h3",{"id":"why-so-many-keys"},"Why So Many Keys?"],["p",{},"Having 3 key pairs for validators adds layers of security and reduces the chance of compromising each key. Validators use their keys to sign blocks, vote for block proposals, and send staking contract transactions."],["p",{},"The terminology of hot, warm, and cold keys outlines the frequency of key usage. The hot key is used more often and readily accessible for regular use. While still used regularly, the warm key is less frequently accessed. On the other hand, the cold key is meant to be kept offline and used less often, making it less susceptible to potential attacks."],["p",{},"The ",["code",{},"update"]," transaction allows the validator to update its keys in case they are compromised. However, the cold key is immutable, and, by extension, so is the validator’s address."]],"toc":{"title":"","searchDepth":2,"depth":2,"links":[{"id":"cold-key","depth":2,"text":"Cold Key"},{"id":"warm-key","depth":2,"text":"Warm Key"},{"id":"hot-key","depth":2,"text":"Hot Key","children":[{"id":"why-so-many-keys","depth":3,"text":"Why So Many Keys?"}]}]}}', 'Validators hold a set of keys in order to participate in the consensus. These transactions include creating a new validator, updating its details, and managing states such as deactivation, reactivation, or retirement. Receipts are issued to authenticate each transaction, ensuring transparency and security. Additionally, if one or more blocks are reverted, these transactions can also be reverted. Below, we describe each transaction and its impact on the validator’s lifecycle."],["h3",{"id":"create"},"Create"],["p",{},"Creates a new validator with an initial deposit that equals the validator''s stake. The deposit is locked in the contract and can only be retrieved by deleting the validator. Validators must provide a signing key, voting key, reward address, and optional signal data."],["h3",{"id":"update"},"Update"],["p",{},"Allows the validator to update details such as the signing key, voting key, reward address, and signal data. The validator must be active to apply these changes."],["h3",{"id":"deactivate"},"Deactivate"],["p",{},"This transaction moves the validator to an inactive state, where it no longer participates in block validation. Deactivation is scheduled to take effect at the next ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/consensus/block-format#macro-blocks"},"election block"],", meaning that the validator continues to be active until the next scheduled election. Deactivation is a necessary step before a validator can be retired or deleted."],["p",{},"Moreover, if a validator remains offline for an extended period, it is deactivated and loses any rewards for the time it remains inactive."],["h3",{"id":"jail"},"Jail"],["p",{},"A validator is jailed immediately after an equivocation proof is submitted accounting for the misbehavior, such as double-signing blocks or attempting to fork the chain. The jailing process also deactivates the validator if it is not already inactive. The validator remains jailed until the jailing period is served, ensuring it cannot participate or tamper with its funds during this time."],["h3",{"id":"reactivate"},"Reactivate"],["p",{},"A validator can send this transaction to reactivate from the inactive state. Once the punishment period has ended (whether penalized for delaying block production or jailed), or if the validator is inactive for other reasons, this transaction reactivates the validator. The validator must meet certain conditions to be reactivated:"],["ul",{},["li",{},"Must be inactive"],["li",{},"Must not be retired"],["li",{},"Must not be jailed at the time of reactivation"]],["p",{},"Reactivation restores the validator''s ability to participate in block validation and earn rewards. Note that this transaction is unnecessary if the validator has the ",["code",{},"automatic_reactivate"]," setting enabled."],["h3",{"id":"retire"},"Retire"],["p",{},"Retiring a validator is an irreversible action that prevents further participation. It is the first step in the process of deleting a validator. Once a validator is retired, it cannot be reactivated. Retirement involves transitioning the validator to an inactive state, if it is still active, and preparing it for eventual deletion."],["h3",{"id":"delete"},"Delete"],["p",{},"This transaction permanently removes a retired validator from the network and returns the validator''s deposit. A validator can only be deleted after completing a cooldown period and if all delegations have been withdrawn. If there are still stakers, a tombstone record is created to track the remaining stake until all stakers withdraw their funds. Tombstones ensure that stakers can retrieve their stake even after the validator is no longer active, safeguarding the network’s economic integrity."],["h3",{"id":"additional-considerations"},"Additional Considerations"],["ol",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Jailing:"]," Even when jailed, validators must continue participating in specific votes (skip blocks and macro blocks) to fulfill the 512 votes requirement and maintain network stability"],["li",{},["strong",{},"State Transition Delays:"]," Most state changes, especially those involving deactivation and reactivation, align with election blocks to prevent disruption during the epoch"],["li",{},["strong",{},"Finality on Retirement:"]," Once a validator enters the retired state, it cannot revert to any other state except deletion, ensuring a straightforward exit process from active validation."]],["h3",{"id":"punishments"},"Punishments"],["p",{},"If a validator delays block production, the ",["strong",{},"penalty"]," is the burning of rewards for that specific block, reducing the validator''s earnings. For more severe offenses, validators face ",["strong",{},"jailing"],", which locks them out of participation for a defined period. During this lock-up period, validators cannot participate in block validation, though they may still be required to participate in voting until the end of the epoch. Validators can only return to active status once the jailing period is served. For more detailed information on punishments, refer to ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/consensus/punishments"},"Nimiq''s punishments documentation"],"."],["h2",{"id":"states"},"States"],["img",{"className":["object-contain","max-h-[max(80vh,220px)]"],"src":"/assets/images/protocol/validator-state.png","alt":"validators states"}],["p",{},["strong",{},"Active:"]," Actively participating in block validation and earning rewards"],["p",{},["strong",{},"Inactive:"]," Temporarily not participating in validation but can be reactivated"],["p",{},["strong",{},"Jailed:"]," Punished for severe misbehavior, temporarily banned from block production, but may still need to participate in voting (skip blocks and macro blocks)"],["p",{},["strong",{},"Retired:"]," Marked for permanent inactivity and pending deletion"],["p",{},["strong",{},"Deleted:"]," Permanently removed from the network, but if there are stakers still associated with the validator, a tombstone is created to manage the remaining stake and stakers until they are cleared"],["h2",{"id":"validator-deletion-process"},"Validator Deletion Process"],["p",{},"Removing a validator is a process that ensures the network has time to detect and report any misbehavior.\nThe following table outlines each transaction involved and the conditions under which they take effect:"],["table",{},["thead",{},["tr",{},["th",{},["strong",{},"Transaction"]],["th",{},["strong",{},"Description"]],["th",{},["strong",{},"Effective Timing"]],["th",{},["strong",{},"Notes"]]]],["tbody",{},["tr",{},["td",{},["strong",{},"Deactivate"]],["td",{},"Marks the validator as inactive."],["td",{},"Takes effect at the start of the next epoch."],["td",{},"Validator cannot be elected in the next epoch. INSERT INTO _content_docs VALUES ('docs/protocol/zkp/prover-node.md', 'Prover Node', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["p",{},"The blockchain uses zero-knowledge proofs to:"],["ul",{},["li",{},"Facilitate light nodes and full nodes to ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/node-sync/macro-sync/light-macro-sync"},"sync"]," with the blockchain"],["li",{},"Enable nodes to reconnect with the blockchain faster"],["li",{},"Secure the network"]],["p",{},"Zero-knowledge proofs prove the chain’s integrity from the genesis block to the latest election macro block in a small proof, which makes it easy for the node receiving it to verify. The proof size remains the same regardless of whether the node requests a proof from the genesis block or the latest macro block."],["p",{},"From the verifier side, receiving and verifying a zero-knowledge proof is a fast process, but generating such proof takes significant computational power and space from the prover node side. Generating these proofs takes time but verifying them is a quick process."],["h2",{"id":"prover-component"},"Prover Component"],["p",{},"Each node in the network has a particular component within its configuration - the ",["a",{"href":"https://github.com/nimiq/core-rs-albatross/blob/ac50167e912b8e36223675495eed1ecd9e226b1c/zkp-component/src/zkp_component.rs","rel":["nofollow"]},"zkp component"],". The component allows nodes to perform multiple tasks such as proof storage, proof of network communication, thus requests and gossip, and optionally proof generation."],["p",{},"But not all nodes need or want to be prover nodes. Being a validator takes considerable computational power, and so does being a prover node. But oppositely to validators, there is no incentive to generate these proofs aside from making the network a more secure and reliable environment."],["p",{},"The prover node component is within the node''s configuration, but the feature is disabled by default. Nodes must activate the feature ",["code",{},"is_prover_active: bool"]," to have the ability to generate a proof. Whenever a node wants to generate a proof, it has to set this feature to ",["code",{},"true"],"."],["h2",{"id":"database"},"Database"],["p",{},"The zkp component also holds a database that allows nodes to store zero-knowledge proofs, preventing losing access to them in the future. The database can be enabled or disabled according to the node choice. Activating the database will allow the node to reconnect from the last zkp in its database."],["p",{},"If a synced node loses connection, it can reconnect by providing the block number of the last zero-knowledge proof stored in its database. But if it doesn''t have its database active and loses connection when reconnecting, it will have to resync from the start of the chain, thus from the genesis block, as it can''t fetch old proofs."],["h2",{"id":"implementation-of-a-zero-knowledge-proof"},"Implementation of a Zero-knowledge Proof"],["p",{},"Zero-knowledge proofs are generated based on three conditions - correctness, soundness, and zero-knowledge. Therefore, by respecting these conditions, the proof attests to the validity of the block production from the genesis block to the respective election block."],["p",{},"There is a possible delay in the zero-knowledge proof generation, where a new election block comes in while the prover node is still generating the proof for the previous election block. Considering this delay, prover nodes provide the latest proof available."],["p",{},"When nodes first sync to the chain, they start at block 0 or genesis block, which is an election block. The genesis block comes with a null proof as it doesn''t contain any element to enable zero-knowledge proof generation. Prover nodes generate these proofs at every election block after the genesis block."],["h2",{"id":"generating-a-zero-knowledge-proof"},"Generating a Zero-knowledge Proof"],["p",{},"The process of generating such proofs is resource-intensive and time-consuming, as it is, for instance, for a history node or a validator node. The prover node generates the proof, stores it in its memory, and propagates it among the network. Note that a zkp can only be generated if it has the feature ",["code",{},"is_prover_node: true"],"."],["p",{},"The proof consists of the last election block number and the proof itself, as follows:"],["pre",{"className":"language-rust shiki shiki-themes material-theme-lighter material-theme material-theme-palenight","code":"pub struct ZKProof {\n    pub block_number: u32,\n    pub proof: Option<Proof<MNT6_753>>,\n}\n","language":"rust","meta":"","style":""},["code",{"__ignoreMap":""},["span",{"class":"line","line":1},["span",{},"pub struct ZKProof {\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":2},["span",{},"    pub block_number: u32,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":3},["span",{},"    pub proof: Option<Proof<MNT6_753>>,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":4},["span",{},"}\n"]]]],["ul",{},["li",{},"The ",["code",{},"block_number"]," consists of the most recent election block. This number must be greater than the node''s number in the request."],["li",{},"The ",["code",{},"proof"]," consists of the zero-knowledge proof generated using a construction based on the MNT4-753 and MNT6-753 elliptic curves."]],["p",{},"In case of a first connection with the network, the node provides the ",["code",{},"block_number"]," 0 as the most recent proof, despite the genesis block having no proof attached to it."],["p",{},"In the case of a node that was synced and had a zero-knowledge proof for election block 43200 in its database, the resync method would differ. If it gets disconnected by chance, when reconnecting again, this node can try to resync with the chain by requesting a zkp more recent than block 43200. However, if this same node hadn''t activated the database feature, it would be unable to resync from the same point and have to sync from the genesis block."],["p",{},"Note that both cases depend on the type of node syncing, as sync with a zero-knowledge proof only applies to light nodes and full nodes."],["h2",{"id":"requesting-a-zero-knowledge-proof"},"Requesting a Zero-knowledge Proof"],["p",{},"The requesting node, rather than downloading and verifying all election macro blocks since the genesis block, can verify the chain''s validity with a zero-knowledge proof upon request. Once a node receives a proof, it first verifies it and then stores it using the zkp component and enabling the database feature."],["p",{},"Whether a node is syncing for the first time or reconnecting to the network, it must request proof to be up-to-date. The request looks as follows:"],["code-group",{},["pre",{"className":"language-rust shiki shiki-themes material-theme-lighter material-theme material-theme-palenight","code":"pub struct RequestZKP {\n    pub(crate) block_number: u32,\n    pub(crate) request_election_block: bool,\n}\n","language":"rust","meta":"","style":""},["code",{"__ignoreMap":""},["span",{"class":"line","line":1},["span",{},"pub struct RequestZKP {\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":2},["span",{},"    pub(crate) block_number: u32,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":3},["span",{},"    pub(crate) request_election_block: bool,\n"]],["span",{"class":"line","line":4},["span",{},"}\n"]]]]],["ul",{},["li",{},"The ",["code",{},"block_number"]," expected is the last election block the node knows of."],["li",{},"If the ",["code",{},"request_election_block"]," is ",["code",{},"true"],", the node expects to receive the complete election block; if set to ",["code",{},"false"],", the node expects to receive the most recent election block number and the proof."]],["h3",{"id":"syncing-to-the-blockchain"},"Syncing to the Blockchain"],["ol",{},["li",{},"node connects to the network"],["li",{},"node requests a zkp by providing the genesis block as ",["code",{},"block_number"]],["li",{},"node verifies the zkp"],["li",{},"node stores the zkp"],["li",{},"node continues the syncing process"]],["h3",{"id":"resyncing-to-the-blockchain"},"Resyncing to the Blockchain"],["ol",{},["li",{},"node reconnects to the network"],["li",{},"node requests a zkp by providing the ",["code",{},"block_number"]," of the latest zkp in its database"],["li",{},"node verifies the zkp"],["li",{},"node stores the zkp"],["li",{},"node continues the syncing process"]],["h4",{"id":"see-also"},"See Also"],["p",{},"To complete the prover node setup, you''ll need the proving keys.
