INSERT INTO _content_docs VALUES ('docs/protocol/zkp/ZKP-and-recursive-SNARKs.md', 'ZKP and Recursive SNARKs', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["h2",{"id":"zero-knowledge-proofs"},"Zero-knowledge Proofs"],["p",{},"A zero-knowledge proof is a mathematical protocol developed to prove a statement without revealing the secret behind the statement. It occurs between two parties: a prover and a verifier. The prover knows a secret and creates a statement about the secret. The verifier can request a proof from the prover, and such proof enables the verification that the prover’s statement is true."],["p",{},"An example of a zero-knowledge proof from ",["a",{"href":"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HUs1bH85X9I","rel":["nofollow"]},"Computerphile''s"]," video: Let''s imagine that Alice wants to prove to Bob she holds a red card that she took from a 52-card deck. Alice is the prover, and Bob is the verifier. Alice knows which card she took, and its suit and index are the secret. Bob only needs to verify that she took a red card. He doesn''t need to know the secret to verify Alice''s statement. Note that, to prove such a statement, Alice has to commit to telling the truth, or the experiment isn''t accurate."],["p",{},"To prove to Bob that Alice took a red card from a 52-card deck, she shows Bob 26 black cards from that deck. Now Bob can verify that Alice is telling the truth. Bob knows that a deck of cards contains 26 red cards and 26 black cards. So, he can verify that Alice is telling the truth, even though he doesn''t know which red card Alice is holding."],["p",{},"Zero-knowledge proofs have three main properties:"],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Correctness"],": The protocol runs accurately if both parties are honest. Alice can only commit to a true statement. If she didn''t take a red card, she couldn''t state that she had taken the red card. Also, Bob couldn''t say he saw a red card if Alice didn''t lay down 26 black cards."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Soundness"],": The prover can only make a statement about a secret he knows to be true. If Alice didn''t know that a deck of cards has 26 red cards and 26 black cards, she couldn''t state that she holds a red card and lay the 26 black cards to prove her statement."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Zero-knowledge"],": No information about the secret is revealed. The secret remains secret. Alice doesn''t need to reveal the secret to Bob to prove her claim."]],["p",{},"This is an example of a zero-knowledge proof with an interaction between two parties, Alice and Bob. But it''s also possible to prove statements without having back-and-forth communication, and here is where SNARKs come into play."],["h2",{"id":"snarks-and-recursive-snarks"},"SNARKs and Recursive SNARKs"],["p",{},"SNARK stands for succinct non-interactive argument of knowledge, and a recursive SNARK is a proof of a SNARK. SNARKs are a new form of zero-knowledge proof that doesn''t require an interaction between parties. They are succinct, meaning the verifier can quickly verify a proof with a simple request to the prover."],["p",{},"SNARKs function with circuits. Circuits refer to the functions or algorithms that allow a statement to be proven. These circuits can be implemented in blockchains to enable users to connect to the network, allowing them to verify the state of the blockchain."],["p",{},"In the Nimiq blockchain, recursive SNARKs are employed to prove the current state of the blockchain with a small proof, constant in size, and easy to verify."],["h2",{"id":"recursive-snarks-implementation"},"Recursive SNARKs Implementation"],["p",{},"To streamline the proof generation for connecting to the network, a specialized type of node is introduced - the ",["a",{"href":"/protocol/zkp/prover-node"},"prover node"],". Prover nodes are designed to efficiently generate the proofs required for establishing the validity of the blockchain''s state without the need for the entire software download."],["h3",{"id":"why-use-recursive-snarks"},"Why use Recursive SNARKs?"],["p",{},"The main purpose of this implementation is to allow nodes to connect to the blockchain easily without downloading the entire software. This validates the current state of the blockchain by proving that validators have produced the blocks according to the consensus rules.\nAlthough SNARKs return a small and easy-to-verify proof for a user like a light node, they consume a lot of computational resources to be generated. As a result, the implementation of recursive SNARKs enables the proof of the previous proof constantly and, in our case, proves the validity of the last epoch with a proof produced in the current one. This way, a chain of recursive SNARKs is created."],["p",{},"Consequently, the proof remains the same size, independently of the size of the blockchain. The proof will be the same size if a light node requests the proof of the interval between the genesis block and the third macro block or the interval between the genesis block and the fiftieth macro block."],["p",{},"Validators produce a proof at each election macro block. Since the validator list remains the same in each epoch, it''s plausible to skip proofs of the batches within an epoch. Each election macro block contains a proof that proves that the last epoch was correctly constructed."],["p",{},"Light and full nodes synchronize to the chain by requesting a proof that returns:"],["ul",{},["li",{},"The last election macro block"],["li",{},"The proof that the chain is valid between the genesis block to the most recent election macro block"]],["p",{},"With this proof, the syncing node is able to verify the chain''s validity between the genesis block and the most recent election macro block. The node only needs to verify the micro and the checkpoint macro blocks produced after this verified election macro block."],["p",{},"Circuits are what allow the setup of these proofs, with a single interaction between the syncing node and the network. These circuits work by pairing two keys: a verifying key and a proving key. They work similarly to public and private keys. The verifying key is stored in the syncing node''s database and allows it to connect to the network. The proving key, downloaded by the prover node, works with the verifying key to establish the connection. By using the verifying key, nodes can request and verify the current state of the blockchain."],["p",{},"To understand the technical part of circuits, check our GitHub ",["a",{"href":"https://github.com/nimiq/core-rs-albatross/tree/albatross/zkp-circuits","rel":["nofollow"]},"repository"],"."],["h2",{"id":"zero-knowledge-proof-setup-ceremony"},"Zero-knowledge Proof Setup Ceremony"],["p",{},"The ceremony generates the proving and verifying keys for ZKPs using a multi-party computation (MPC) model. It involves multiple participants who contribute randomness to create these keys, enhancing security and preventing any single point of failure."],["ul",{},["li",{},["strong",{},"Phase 1"],": Generates generic parameters called Powers of Tau, which are specific to the cryptographic curve and ZKP system."],["li",{},["strong",{},"Phase 2"],": Refines these parameters for the specific ZKP circuits, ultimately generating the proving and verifying keys."]],["p",{},"This process ensures that the setup remains secure and resistant to compromise. For more details about the circuits, refer to this ",["a",{"href":"https://github.com/nimiq/core-rs-albatross/blob/albatross/zkp-circuits/README.md","rel":["nofollow"]},"README file"],"."]],"toc":{"title":"","searchDepth":2,"depth":2,"links":[{"id":"zero-knowledge-proofs","depth":2,"text":"Zero-knowledge Proofs"},{"id":"snarks-and-recursive-snarks","depth":2,"text":"SNARKs and Recursive SNARKs"},{"id":"recursive-snarks-implementation","depth":2,"text":"Recursive SNARKs Implementation","children":[{"id":"why-use-recursive-snarks","depth":3,"text":"Why use Recursive SNARKs?"}]},{"id":"zero-knowledge-proof-setup-ceremony","depth":2,"text":"Zero-knowledge Proof Setup Ceremony"}]}}', 'Nimiq supports several types of users and syncing mechanisms. Light and full nodes can synchronize to the network by requesting a proof of the current state of the blockchain. The blockchain implements zero-knowledge proofs capable of proving most of the chain in a small proof.', 'md', 'i-tabler:lock', NULL, '{"matter":{"title":"ZKP and Recursive SNARKs","description":"Nimiq supports several types of users and syncing mechanisms. Light and full nodes can synchronize to the network by requesting a proof of the current state of the blockchain. The blockchain implements zero-knowledge proofs capable of proving most of the chain in a small proof."}}', '{"title":"ZKP and Recursive SNARKs","order":1}', '/protocol/zkp/zkp-and-recursive-snarks', '{"title":"ZKP and Recursive SNARKs","description":"Nimiq supports several types of users and syncing mechanisms. Which is more than the f slots that are not in this group (which must all\nbe honest).\nIt is calculated as ",["code",{},"ceil(SLOTS*2/3)"]," and we use the formula ",["code",{},"ceil(x/y) = (x+y-1)/y"]," for the\nceiling division."],["hr",{}],["h3",{"id":"validator_deposit"},"VALIDATOR_DEPOSIT"],["blockquote",{},["p",{},["code",{},"readonly"]," ",["code",{},"static"]," ",["strong",{},"VALIDATOR_DEPOSIT"],": ",["code",{},"bigint"]]],["p",{},"Defined in: @nimiq/core/types/wasm/web.d.ts:1679"],["p",{},"The deposit necessary to create a validator in Lunas (1 NIM = 100,000 Lunas).\nA validator is someone who actually participates in block production. Nimiq transactions can have one of two formats: \"basic\" and \"extended\".\nBasic transactions are simple value transfers between two regular address types and cannot contain\nany extra data. Basic transactions can be serialized to less bytes, so take up less place on the\nblockchain. Extended transactions on the other hand are all other transactions: contract creations\nand interactions, staking transactions, transactions with exta data, etc."],["hr",{}],["h3",{"id":"network"},"network"],["blockquote",{},["p",{},["strong",{},"network"],": ",["code",{},"string"]]],["p",{},"Defined in: @nimiq/core/types/wasm/web.d.ts:522"],["p",{},"The network name on which this transaction is valid."],["hr",{}],["h3",{"id":"proof"},"proof"],["blockquote",{},["p",{},["strong",{},"proof"],": ",["a",{"href":"../type-aliases/PlainTransactionProof"},["code",{},"PlainTransactionProof"]]]],["p",{},"Defined in: @nimiq/core/types/wasm/web.d.ts:546"],["p",{},"The ",["code",{},"proof"]," field contains the signature of the eligible signer. INSERT INTO _content_docs VALUES ('docs/web-client/web-client-vs-rpc.md', 'Web Client vs RPC Client', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["h2",{"id":"tldr"},"TL;DR"],["p",{},"The Nimiq Web Client establishes direct connections with other peers in the network, making it a part of the blockchain network. In contrast, the RPC follows a client-server model, where clients connect to a single server that interacts with multiple peers on the network. The Web Client operates in JavaScript environments, such as web browsers and Node.js, while the RPC can be used from any programming language and environment through HTTP calls."],["img",{"className":["object-contain","max-h-[max(60vh,180px)]"],"src":"/assets/images/protocol/network.png","alt":"Web Client vs RPC"}],["h2",{"id":"nimiq-web-client"},"Nimiq Web Client"],["p",{},"The ",["a",{"href":"/web-client/"},"Nimiq Web Client"]," is a JavaScript library that enables direct participation in the Nimiq PoS ecosystem. It can run in environments including web browsers as well as server-side environments like Node.js. This client is compiled from Rust to WebAssembly using ",["code",{},"wasm-pack"]," and offers a lightweight solution for users who want to interact with the Nimiq blockchain."],["p",{},"The Web Client provides a user-friendly interface for building consensus with other nodes, creating wallets, sending transactions, and interacting with the blockchain through simple operations. It simplifies user interaction and offers easy integration."],["p",{},"Users can access the Web Client on mobile devices, such as smartphones and tablets, providing flexibility and convenience for managing their Nimiq wallets and transactions on the go. The Web Client is designed to be compatible with major web browsers, including Google Chrome, Mozilla Firefox, Microsoft Edge, and Safari, ensuring a consistent user experience across different platforms."],["h2",{"id":"nimiq-rpc"},"Nimiq RPC"],["p",{},["a",{"href":"/rpc/"},"Nimiq RPC"]," provides developers access to a node that is part of the Nimiq blockchain and establishes a connection between a client and a single server, which then interacts with multiple peers on the network."],["p",{},"RPC offers more elaborate functionalities, such as retrieving information about the blockchain state, submitting transactions, managing accounts, and configuring node settings. It is particularly valuable for developers and nodes who require advanced control and automation of blockchain operations."],["p",{},"RPC supports interaction with nodes and allows users to call its endpoints using HTTP from any language or software capable of sending requests according to the JSON-RPC specification. Comprehensive API documentation is available for the RPC, detailing the methods, parameters, and endpoints supported by the interface, facilitating seamless integration and development."],["h2",{"id":"comparison-table"},"Comparison Table"],["table",{},["thead",{},["tr",{},["th",{},"Aspect"],["th",{},"Web Client"],["th",{},"RPC Client"]]],["tbody",{},["tr",{},["td",{},["strong",{},"Use Cases"]],["td",{},"Nimiq Wallet, Nimiq Pay, browser dApps"],["td",{},"TrustWallet, Block Explorer, server applications"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["strong",{},"Accessibility"]],["td",{},"Requires familiarity with JavaScript and similar frameworks like Node.js; can be run in a browser"],["td",{},"Requires adherence to the JSON-RPC specification"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["strong",{},"Functionality"]],["td",{},"Offers lightweight access to blockchain operations and functions as a light node within the network"],["td",{},"Offers full node control, providing a wider range of options, particularly for managing full and history nodes"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["strong",{},"User Base"]],["td",{},"For developers capable of managing consensus within a JavaScript environment"],["td",{},"For developers seeking to retrieve data efficiently within a stable client and manage their node"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["strong",{},"Infrastructure"]],["td",{},"No server infrastructure required"],["td",{},"Requires running a Nimiq node"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["strong",{},"Performance"]],["td",{},"Peer-to-peer, direct blockchain access"],["td",{},"Centralized through your node"]],["tr",{},["td",{},["strong",{},"Languages"]],["td",{},"JavaScript/TypeScript"],["td",{},"Any language with HTTP support"]]]],["h2",{"id":"when-to-use-each"},"When to Use Each"],["h3",{"id":"choose-web-client-when"},"Choose Web Client When"],["ul",{},["li",{},"Building browser-based applications"],["li",{},"Creating decentralized applications (dApps)"],["li",{},"Want to minimize infrastructure requirements"],["li",{},"Need mobile-friendly solutions"],["li",{},"Building with JavaScript/TypeScript"]],["h3",{"id":"choose-rpc-client-when"},"Choose RPC Client When"],["ul",{},["li",{},"Building server-side applications"],["li",{},"Need advanced node management"],["li",{},"Working with languages other than JavaScript"],["li",{},"Require high-performance data retrieval"],["li",{},"Building block explorers or analytics tools"]],["h2",{"id":"what-you-can-do"},"What You Can Do"],["h3",{"id":"web-client-capabilities"},"Web Client Capabilities"],["p",{},"For users seeking a straightforward and user-friendly way to interact with the blockchain, the Nimiq Web Client is the ideal choice. With its intuitive interface, users can perform basic operations such as creating wallets, sending transactions, and checking balances within their browsers. The Web Client simplifies blockchain interactions, making it accessible to users of all skill levels. Some of the operations supported by the Web Client include:"],["ul",{},["li",{},"Create new wallet addresses"],["li",{},"Send transactions to other addresses"],["li",{},"View transaction history"],["li",{},"Check account balances"],["li",{},"Subscribe to real-time blockchain events"]],["h3",{"id":"rpc-capabilities"},"RPC Capabilities"],["p",{},"For developers, tech-savvy users, and validators looking for more advanced control over blockchain operations, the Nimiq RPC provides extensive capabilities for accessing their nodes. Through established methods and endpoints, users can perform a wide range of operations, including:"],["ul",{},["li",{},"Lock and unlock accounts"],["li",{},"Verify transactions"],["li",{},"List all available accounts and their balances"],["li",{},"Retrieve ZKP state information"],["li",{},"Manage validator operations"],["li",{},"Advanced blockchain querying"]],["h2",{"id":"start-building-now"},"Start Building Now"],["p",{},"Now that you know the differences, why not start building?"],["u-page-grid",{},["u-page-card",{"icon":"i-lucide-globe","title":"Web Client","to":"../web-client","variant":"outline"}],["u-page-card",{"icon":"i-lucide-terminal","title":"Nimiq RPC","to":"../rpc","variant":"outline"}],["u-page-card",{"icon":"i-lucide-shield-check","title":"Validators","to":"../nodes/validators/becoming-a-validator","variant":"outline"}]],["h2",{"id":"learn-more"},"Learn More"],["p",{},"Curious about how the Nimiq blockchain works?
